title: Elmwood Systems — Retainer Uplift & EU on-prem motion
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-11-01
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~48 minutes
Participants: Maya Thompson (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Sofia Alvarez (Elmwood - Head of ML Ops), Tom Reed (Elmwood - VP Eng), Priya Nair (Elmwood - Security)
Links: pricing spreadsheet (shared), draft runbook (to be shared), SOC2 package link (requested)

Auto-generated summary (may be partial):
Elmwood is evaluating a 12-month retainer uplift to reserve Dedicated capacity and run a short EU on-prem pilot for data residency. Primary concerns: integration with their K M S, SOC two evidence, latency targets for live inference, and cost delta with quantization. Redwood AE proposes retainer + pilot, SE to supply on-prem checklist and runbook. Next steps agreed: pricing, runbook, pilot SLOs, and technical deep-dive.

Topics covered:
- Retainer uplift size and billing cadence
- EU on‑prem pilot requirements and timeline
- Security/compliance (SOC two, audit logs, K M S)
- Performance: latency SLOs, throughput, KV cache behavior
- Follow‑ups and pilot acceptance criteria

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya (Redwood): hey everyone thanks for making the time, i'll kick off — quick agenda: pricing, pilot, security Q's, then next steps. sound good?
[00:12] Sofia (Elmwood): yeah thanks Maya, appreciate it. we just want to nail timelines — our board wants region residency by end of q1.
[00:20] Tom (Elmwood): and also want to understand cost delta if we go dedicated + on‑prem vs hosted with strict egress controls.
[00:28] Maya: totally understood. Ethan's on for the on‑prem details but high level: we usually recommend a 12‑month retainer for predictable throughput then a 6‑8 week on‑prem pilot for validation.
[00:45] Ethan (Redwood SE): hi all, quick note — for on‑prem we ship a control plane that can sit in your V P C, or we do an air‑gapped package if needed. K V cache and prefix caching behavior will be similar but we'll need to confirm network egress and K M S connectivity.
[01:05] Priya (Elmwood): when you say K M S connectivity, do you mean we have to open outbound to redwood KMS endpoints? that's a non‑starter for us.
[01:15] Ethan: no, we can integrate with your existing K M S via a customer‑managed HSM or we can use a locally deployed key proxy. so, no external key egress required for the keys themselves. we'll document options.
[01:28] Sofia: okay good. also can you confirm audit logs — we need per‑request logs for a 30‑day retention window in EU.
[01:36] Maya: yes we provide audit logs and can increase retention per contract, additional retention is an add‑on but we can include it in the retainer proposal.
[01:45] Tom: curious on performance — if we quantize model to reduce cost, what latency hit do we expect?
[01:54] Ethan: depends on model and quantisation profile. typical 8‑bit quant gives 1.1x token latency or thereabouts on the same GPU family, but throughput improves. for tail latency we add small batching windows, and we have a continuous batching runtime that keeps p99s predictable.
[02:12] Sofia: you said 1.1x, our p95 target is 250ms for single‑turn customer facing calls. is that feasible in the EU on‑prem env?
[02:21] Ethan: with right hardware (A100 class / equivalent) and tuned quantization, yes. but the pilot will validate p95. we'll set SLOs in the pilot runbook.
[02:35] Maya: i'll add a note for pilot SLOs and expected hw profiles.
[02:40] Tom: what about fallbacks — if on‑prem spikes or we need additional capacity, can we burst to hosted?
[02:48] Ethan: yes, hybrid routing is supported. policy can route by latency or cost, and we can configure automatic fallback to hosted instances in another region. for residency we ensure data is retained in EU, and bursts are only allowed if you permit cross‑zone egress.
[03:04] Priya: we might not allow cross‑zone egress for customer PII; so bursting would be limited to model‑only traffic or non‑PII.
[03:11] Maya: that makes sense — we'll document allowed burst modes and masking options.
[03:20] Sofia: regarding contract length — our procurement prefers annual commitments. what are the incentives for a 12‑month vs 24‑month?
[03:29] Maya: typically 12 vs 24 we provide stepped discounts: 12 months has X% off list for reserved throughput; 24 months adds further discount and committed burst credits. i'll share a matrix.
[03:40] Tom: can you include a path to increase capacity midterm without penalty? sometimes usage surprises us.
[03:47] Maya: yes, we have midterm top‑ups; pricing will be pro‑rata and we can lock rates for the remainder of the term.
[03:55] Ethan: operationally we provision nodes in the dedicated pool — scale up is usually fast if capacity available; for on‑prem adding nodes depends on your infra window.
[04:12] Sofia: timeline question — we need EU residency by end of q1. is that doable if we start pilot now?
[04:20] Ethan: timeline, rough estimate: 2 weeks to provision reserved capacity, 4–6 weeks for on‑prem install and validation, then 2 weeks for pilot acceptance. so from kickoff it's ~8–10 weeks, which could meet end‑of‑q1 if we start in November.
[04:37] Priya: fine, but we need SOC two artifacts before pilot. do you have a SOC two package?
[04:44] Maya: yes we are SOC two Type 2 certified. we'll send the SOC two package and the compliance checklist. note: redwood will redact customer‑specific items in the package.
[04:58] Tom: procurement wants to see SLAs for p99 availability for Dedicated. can you commit?
[05:06] Maya: yes, dedicated comes with SLA tiers; p99 availability targets are in the enterprise addendum. we'll include SLA language for the retainer term.
[05:16] Sofia: one more technical: how do you manage KV cache persistence on on‑prem? we rely on cached state between calls.
[05:25] Ethan: KV cache is local to the serving nodes, for on‑prem you can enable sticky sessions or shared redis persistence. performance tradeoff is small but shared storage adds a bit of tail latency.
[05:38] Priya: is that redis managed by us or by you?
[05:41] Ethan: either — we can operate a managed redis in your V P C under a managed ops contract, or you manage it and we provide configs.
[05:50] Sofia: okay. i want to call out data flow — can you capture that in the runbook so our security team sees egress points?
[05:58] Ethan: yes we'll include network diagrams, ports, and example flow with K M S interaction.
[06:07] Tom: on billing, do you support invoicing through our procurement system, or only card?
[06:11] Maya: for enterprise retainer we invoice via PO and net terms; smaller pilots can be on card/credit.
[06:19] Sofia: what's the expected delta in cost if we pick dedicated+on‑prem vs hosted? ballpark.
[06:25] Maya: ballpark depends a lot on throughput and model choice; rough example: for 100M tokens/month hosted might be cheaper initial, but at sustained high throughput dedicated reduces per‑token cost by ~25–40%. on‑prem adds setup and infra costs — we'll put numbers in the pricing sheet.
[06:44] Tom: can you put a simple excel scenario: hosted vs dedicated vs dedicated+on‑prem with our numbers?
[06:49] Maya: yes i'll populate with your numbers once you confirm expected throughput.
[06:55] Priya: regarding encryption keys, our legal insists keys never leave our control. confirm again — redwood agent must not have key access.
[07:03] Ethan: confirmed — we support customer‑managed keys where the key material never leaves your K M S. the control plane can store references only.
[07:10] Sofia: are there any blockers on your side for ISO or data‑residency audits?
[07:15] Maya: we can provide evidence and support audits; details depend on the enterprise package. we'll work with your compliance lead.
[07:22] Tom: i'd like to carve out an initial 4‑week pilot acceptance criteria: p95 < 250ms, availability 99.5, successful K M S integration, and log retention validated. can you draft that?
[07:33] Ethan: yes we'll include these as part of pilot SLOs.
[07:38] Sofia: for the pilot — what do you need from us to start?
[07:42] Ethan: a small infra window: VM or k8s nodes, network policy to allow internal control plane comms, K M S test profile, and sample traffic to model latency. plus an internal owner for the pilot.
[07:54] Tom: I can own the infra piece, Sofia will own pilots metrics.
[07:59] Maya: great. i'll follow up with an email summarizing owners and timeline.
[08:05] Priya: about SOC two evidence — i'll send the list of compliance docs we need; can you also provide a data flow for bursts?
[08:12] Ethan: yes, we'll include burst flow diagrams and options for masking PII.
[08:18] Sofia: two quick competitive notes — we're also talking to Apex and CloudModelX, they push lower list for hosted but not sure on on‑prem.
[08:26] Maya: understood — we'll make the on‑prem case: predictable latency, compliance, and integrated K M S.
[08:32] Tom: can we get a short notes deck for execs that summarizes cost vs compliance tradeoffs?
[08:37] Maya: yes i'll include a 1‑pager.
[08:40] Sofia: great. any other questions?
[08:44] Tom: one last thing — do you support function/tool calling with on‑prem runtime? some of our flows call external services.
[08:51] Ethan: yes function calling is supported; calls can be proxied through your network so external calls follow your egress policy.
[08:59] Maya: ok, to recap: we'll send updated pricing and matrix, Ethan will share on‑prem runbook and K M S options, Priya will send compliance doc list, Tom to confirm throughput numbers. target next meeting: tech runbook deep‑dive week of Nov 8.
[09:15] Sofia: that works. thanks everyone.

End of transcript.

Recap / Meeting closeout note:
- Decision drivers: compliance (K M S, SOC two), predictable latency (p95 target 250ms), cost optimization via dedicated and quantization.
- Pilot scope: EU on‑prem install + limited traffic validation; acceptance SLOs to be documented.
- Commercial: 12‑month retainer recommended; 24‑month optional for bigger discount.

References / shared artifacts:
- Pricing spreadsheet: link (shared during call)
- Placeholder: on‑prem runbook (Ethan to upload)
- SOC two package: requested by Priya

Explicit next steps (also in action_items):
- Maya to send pricing and exec 1‑pager (due 2027-11-03)
- Ethan to draft and share on‑prem runbook and K M S integration patterns (due 2027-11-04)
- Tom to confirm throughput numbers and infra windows (due 2027-11-05)
- Priya to send compliance doc checklist (due 2027-11-06)

Notes on transcription: occasional mis‑heard acronyms (K M S / SOC two) and numeric rounding.

title: Hosted Gen Onramp — stream resilience & param workshop
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-01-15
Start time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 58 minutes (approx)
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Priya Nair (Velox CTO), Jamal Torres (Velox Lead ML Engineer), Aisha Khan (Velox DevOps), Marco (Velox PM)

Auto-generated summary (brief):
Quick hosted API onramp covering streaming behavior, generation params (temperature, top_p, max_tokens), retry/backoff strategies, and a console tour for monitoring/Optimize suggestions. Customer asked about latency vs cost tradeoffs, VPC options, and audit logs. Agreed next steps to run latency benchmarks and share security docs + pricing/pilot paperwork.

Topics: 
- Streaming semantics and partial chunks
- Retry/backoff patterns and idempotency keys
- Param knobs (temperature, top_p, stop sequences, max_tokens) and defaults for retail chat flows
- Console demo: per-model latency breakdown, token billing, Optimize suggestions (batching/quantization)
- Security: SOC2, SSO/SAML, KMS, VPC routing

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya Chen: hey everyone thanks for joining, i'm maya from redwood, i'll run through a short demo then ethan will dive into the streaming and resilience bits, and we can surface any infra/security constraints at the end
[00:16] Priya Nair: hi thanks, i've, uh, Priya here. we wanted to see concrete behavior when users type and to under-stand how streaming increments arrive and how that affects perceived latency
[00:30] Ethan Park: great, so we'll start with the hosted gen chat endpoint, small note — streaming is chunked by token groups and the client sees partial deltas, so you can show UI tokens earlier, it reduces time-to-first-byte, um, but there's backpressure knobs in the client as well
[00:52] Jamal Torres: sorry can i interrupt — when you say chunked by token groups are those fixed sizes or content dependent
[01:05] Ethan Park: good question. it's content dependent; the runtime groups tokens into emission windows influenced by model tokenization and internal batching. in practice we see chunks ~8-32 tokens but it varies with long sequences
[01:24] Aisha Khan: and on the billing side each token chunk is still counted, right? like is there any hidden extra cost because of streaming
[01:34] Maya Chen: correct, you are billed per token generated/consumed, streaming doesn't double bill, it's the same token accounting, you only pay for tokens. small note: prefix caching can reduce repeated tokens if you have similar prompts
[01:49] Marco (PM): can you show the console where we can see token costs per route and per model? that's critical for us
[01:58] Maya Chen: sure, switching to the console — bear with me, i'm sharing my screen — top left you'll see per-route cost and p95 latency, we also surface token breakdown by prompt vs. completion
[02:20] [Screen demo noise - brief silence] 
[02:35] Ethan Park: jumping back to resilience — retries. the common pattern we recommend: client sets idempotency key per user-turn, set conservative retry with exponential backoff and jitter, and use server-side de-dup checks. Redwood will detect duplicate idempotency keys within a window and avoid double-execution
[03:02] Priya Nair: window as in time window? what about partial completions if client reconnects mid-stream
[03:14] Ethan Park: yes time window; default 120s but configurable. if reconnect happens mid-stream the client can either resume with a continuation token or request a fresh generation. resume requires the same idempotency and a resume token that ties to the KV cache — if resume not possible we fall back to re-gen with the same seed when available
[03:38] Jamal Torres: you mentioned KV cache — can you explain that, we use a lot of context windows, esp product descriptions + inventory state
[03:48] Ethan Park: the KV cache stores recent key/value attention states for ongoing sessions to avoid re-computing prefixes; it speeds up repeated short-turn chats a lot. in hosted we've optimized eviction and we expose controls in the console — you can pin prefixes or set TTLs
[04:12] Aisha Khan: is that state stored on our side or Redwood? data residency is a must for us — we need to know if PII could be kept 
[04:26] Maya Chen: good call — for hosted it's stored encrypted at rest by Redwood, accessible only to the runtime. we support customer-managed KMS for Dedicated/Private; for hosted we have KMS with separation of roles and audit logs. we'll share the security whitepaper after this call
[04:45] Priya Nair: do you have SOC2? we also need SSO, role based access
[04:52] Maya Chen: yes SOC 2 type II. SSO/SAML is supported, we have RBAC in the console with token scoping and audit trails
[05:05] [00:05 minute mark — a short pause while Maya toggles screens]

[05:52] Jamal Torres: for our checkout assistant what would safe defaults be? low hallucination, deterministic price lookups, short completions
[06:04] Ethan Park: for transactional flows i suggest temp 0.0-0.2, top_p 0.8, set a strict max_tokens of 60 and include stop sequences to prevent trailing commentary. also add a post-filter step against product database to validate that the model references SKU ids that exist

[06:35] Maya Chen: yes, i'll make a quick request from the playground — note this is a sandbox payload so tokens will be low. starting request now
[06:50] [live generation starts]

[07:20] Ethan Park: you might notice small pauses where the runtime is batching other requests, that's the batching window optimizing throughput — you can lower batching to favor latency or increase to favor cost per token
[07:41] Priya Nair: is there a recommended batching config for 200 QPS with small responses like 30 tokens?
[07:53] Ethan Park: for short completions at 200 QPS we usually pick smaller batch windows ~10-20ms and enable prefix caching. the console Optimize tab will actually suggest batch targets after a short sampling period
[08:15] Aisha Khan: how about quantization? i heard you have quantized model offerings that lower cost but might affect latency
[08:26] Ethan Park: we do. quantized variants (8-bit/4-bit) reduce memory and inference cost and often improve throughput, but they can change model quality slightly. Optimize will recommend quantization only if quality regressions are within your guardrails
[08:45] Jamal Torres: can Optimize run our eval prompts against candidate quantized models automatically

[11:58] Maya Chen: the console has a cost estimator; Optimize runs suggestions that include cost impact. common pattern: keep customer-facing baseline on hosted with conservative batch settings and fall back to Dedicated when you need guaranteed throughput for flash events

[12:25] Ethan Park: yes Private offering runs in-customer VPC or on-prem. network setup involves VPC peering or PrivateLink; we ship an agent and the control plane is optionally customer-managed. full audit trail and integration with your KMS/HSM are supported in Private

[14:55] Aisha Khan: how do we surface the p99 numbers in the console? we need programmatic access for autoscaling triggers
[15:02] Maya Chen: the console exposes metrics via a metrics API (Prometheus compatible) and you can consume p50/p95/p99 and hook those into your autoscale policies. we can share the API spec and a demo prom exporter

[15:40] Marco (PM): about privacy — if a customer wants to delete user data how quickly can you purge?
[15:48] Maya Chen: hosted supports delete-by-user with purge within 48 hours; Dedicated/Private gives you control to purge faster as you control storage. we'll share the data retention SLA in the security docs
[16:05] Jamal Torres: on the console demo earlier, can you show where fallback policies are configured
[16:12] Ethan Park: sure — under routing policies there's a policy editor. you can make rules by region, model latency, error rates and chain fallback targets. you can also set per-tenant overrides
[16:28] Priya Nair: is there an audit trail for routing changes? we need change history for compliance
[16:34] Maya Chen: yes, all routing edits are auditable with who/when and you can export audit logs to your SIEM via syslog or cloud storage
[16:50] [17:00 minute mark — Q&A on billing granularity] 
[17:10] Aisha Khan: do you support monthly committed usage and discounts?
[17:16] Maya Chen: yes reserved capacity contracts for Dedicated and committed usage discounts for hosted via an enterprise plan. we'll include pricing tiers in the doc I'm sending
[17:30] Jamal Torres: one more technical — if we do a canary and a rollback, is in-flight traffic interrupted or allowed to finish?
[17:39] Ethan Park: in-flight requests will finish on the node they started; routing changes apply to new requests. rollback only affects new traffic and future opens. we also have circuit breakers to avoid islanding nodes under faulty conditions
[17:58] Priya Nair: ok that's good.
[18:05] Maya Chen: quick recap — agreed: Ethan will run the latency sim with 200 concurrent sessions, Priya will send prompt templates and QPS numbers, Maya will send security + pricing and we'll set the follow-up for the Dedicated cost estimate. anything to add?
[18:24] Jamal Torres: just please include sample request payloads for streaming resume tokens and idempotency key examples
[18:33] Maya Chen: will do, i'll attach code snippets (JS + python) and a sample client backoff snippet
[18:43] Marco (PM): thanks, this was super helpful.
[18:48] [call winds down — casual signoffs] 
[19:00] Priya Nair: thanks everyone, talk soon
[19:05] Aisha Khan: bye
[19:09] Maya Chen: thanks all, bye

title: Cascade Interop - HPC GPU Solution Sync
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-12-15
Start: 16:30 UTC
Duration: 54m
Attendees: Ethan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Sofia (Redwood Solutions), Liam (Cascade Head of Platform), Saira (HPC Arch), Jon (Partnerships), Tara (Security)
Shared links: Deck: https://redwood.app/s/interop-deck , Benchmark script: https://redwood.app/s/cascade-bench

Auto-summary (auto): quick partner intro, understand Cascade's HPC GPU hosting model, discuss integration patterns (VPC/private, dedicated GPU pools), alignment on pilot success metrics and next steps.

Topics: partner model, hw topology, networking/VPC, security (KMS/SOC2), benchmark plan, pricing/commit tradeoffs, rollout timeline

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Ethan: All right good afternoon everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Ethan from Redwood, AE on the partnerships team, and with me is Priya our SE who focuses on high throughput GPU serving. Sofia is on for solutions. Tara from Cascade, Saira, Liam, Jon thanks for taking the time.
[00:18] Liam: Yeah thanks, uh appreciate it. Wanted to get a sense of how Redwood would integrate with our HPC stacks, we run a mix of on-prem racks and a couple of co-lo sites, and we also use public cloud for burst.
[00:28] Priya: Cool, can you paint a bit of the topology? like node types, GPUS, interconnect, and are you wanting managed hosted endpoints or private deployment?
[00:34] Saira: Right now we have mostly A100 class on-prem, some new H100 test racks, 200 GPUs total across two data centers, 40Gb infiniband, and we do a custom scheduler. We want Redwood to sit as an inference runtime that either talks to our internal sched or you provide a VPC/private controller.
[00:50] Ethan: Got it. Typical pattern is two options: Dedicated (Redwood-managed reserved pools) or Private deployment where the control plane is customer-managed and runtime runs inside your VPC. For on-prem we can do an air-gapped package.
[01:05] Jon: Air-gapped would be important for a couple of our defense-facing workloads. Also curious about KV cache handling — latency sensitive workloads so how do you keep the kv cache warm across nodes?
[01:15] Priya: Good question. We do continuous batching + local kv co-location where possible; there's also prefix cache in the front proxy. I should call out language here — sometimes the transcript generator writes "kay vee cache" so apologies if that shows up funny in notes.
[01:30] Saira: heh yeah we saw that in previous calls. but concretely, on our side we can't share certain training artifacts so we need guaranteed encryption at rest and KMS integration.
[01:40] Tara: Right, SOC2 and FIPS requirements for a subset. We need to understand log retention, audit logs, and key management flows.
[01:46] Sofia: Understood. Redwood's private deployment supports customer-managed KMS and HSM integrations; audit logs can be shipped to your SIEM and we can support limited retention windows for compliance. We'll share our security runbook.
[01:57] Liam: And what about performance — we've seen big variance between vendors. We need determinism: latency p95 under 50ms for our shorter context workloads. Can Redwood hit that on A100 / H100?
[02:08] Priya: For short contexts and optimized kernels yes we can be sub-50ms p95 on H100 for many models, pending batch sizes. We'll need to baseline with your actual model and sequence lengths. For autoregressive large contexts there's tradeoffs around batching and KV size.
[02:24] Saira: We're using a mix of Llama-derived models and some custom finetunes, roughly 6B-13B sizes for low-latency. Also interested in quantization — do you support int8 or 4-bit paths?
[02:37] Priya: We support quantized execution paths; we have a quantization/compilation flow that picks kernels. For some custom finetunes we recommend a small retrace step to validate quality.
[02:48] Jon: Ok cost levers — can you talk about dedicated vs hosted pricing and burst behavior? cascade wants predictable unit economics.
[02:55] Ethan: Sure. Hosted usage is usage-based with per-token/per-embedding rates. Dedicated is a reserved capacity model with monthly commit. For on-prem private there's a license + support/ops fee. We can design a hybrid where production critical traffic goes to dedicated pools and burst to hosted for spiky loads.
[03:14] Liam: Hmm the burst to hosted is tricky for us because of data egress and some workloads can't leave premises. Any way to keep everything local and still get Redwood updates?
[03:22] Sofia: Yes, we can do a private control plane with local runtime and opt-in telemetry for updates, or an offline upgrade bundle for air-gapped infra.
[03:32] Tara: Security quibble — encryption in transit is a given but we need customer-controlled keys for persistence. Also RBAC for teams and token scoping.
[03:40] Priya: We support SAML/SSO for RBAC, token scoping, and KMS integration for key control. We'll add the exact flow into the security doc.
[03:48] [04:00] Ethan: Let's sketch a pilot. Typical minimal pilot is a two-node cluster in your co-lo with a couple of representative models, run the benchmark, validate latency and correctness, then a short 30-day prod trial. Priya will run the bench.
[04:12] Liam: Sounds sensible. Timeboxed — we'd like proof within 2 weeks.
[04:16] Priya: We'll aim to run the benchmark in 4 business days after we get the dataset and access — so 2026-12-20 target for results.
[04:24] Saira: Access — we prefer to keep data on-prem. Could you provide a bench harness we can run internally and then upload the perf artifacts?
[04:31] Priya: Yep that's what the benchmark script link is for. We'll also provide an auth wrapper so you don't have to expose credentials.
[04:37] Jon: A few more technicals — how does the runtime handle model rollouts? Can we pin versions and do canary traffic splitting?

preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-12-14
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~58 minutes
Attendees: Sofia Martinez (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Maya Li (Redwood CSM), Rahim Patel (Coraline Head of ML), Diane Cho (CTO), Omar Reyes (SRE), Lina Ghosh (Finance)

Summary: quick ops + econ handoff after trial phase; confirm pilot sizing, security requirements, cost/latency tradeoffs; agree next steps for dedicated vs hosted evaluation and benchmark plan.

Topics: usage trending, cost drivers, reliability incidents (two spikes), model variants and seq length distribution, KV cache + batching settings, security (SOC2, SSO, KMS), pilot timeline and acceptance criteria.

Transcript:
[00:00] Sofia: hey everyone, thanks for joining — I think we have about an hour, we wanted to do a quick ops and econ handoff given your trial and the spike last week. hi Rahim, Diane.
[00:18] Rahim: yeah thanks, uh, thanks. quick note: Lina's on for finance Qs, Omar is here for infra. we, um, saw two notable latency spikes on Tuesday around 11:30 PST, that triggered our pager.
[00:34] Omar: yeah, the SRE runbook kicked in but we still lost some tail performance. not sure if it was batching config or cold starts on the Dedicated nodes.
[00:47] Ethan: got it. we dug into the logs — looks like a mix: there were a couple of large requests with 2k+ tokens, KV cache misses spiking, and then a concurrent burst. our continuous batching can absorb some but with big seqs it...uh...it can push the latency.
[01:05] Diane: can you clarify — is that an argument for Hosted or Dedicated? because finance is looking at unit costs and we need predictability.
[01:17] Lina: yes, predictable monthly is preferred; spikes cost us overtime dev cycles.
[01:23] Sofia: totally. so the tradeoff is predictable throughput (Dedicated/reserved) vs lower up-front (Hosted pay-as-you-go). Dedicated gives you pinned pools and autoscale rules we can tune. Hosted benefits from cross-customer multitenancy which can be cheaper at low steady-state but less predictable during big bursts.
[01:46] Rahim: if we did Dedicated, are we able to keep the same model version pinning and can we do canary rollouts? we'd want the same rollout controls.
[01:57] Ethan: yes, same API surface; you can pin model versions, do canary percentages, rollbacks. the only difference is the underlying pool is isolated to you and we can guarantee throughput SLOs.
[02:10] Omar: security question — do we get full KMS integration and customer-managed keys? our compliance team needs HSM/KMS and audit logs for model inputs.
[02:20] Maya: short answer: yes for Private and Dedicated we support KMS integrations and audit logs. for Hosted we'll push logs to retained storage but for the strictest requirements we recommend Private (VPC/on-prem). we'll share the security pack.
[02:36] Diane: do you have SOC2 and ISO docs? our procurement requires both before contract.
[02:42] Sofia: yes, SOC2 Type II and ISO27001 — I'll attach them. also our security whitepaper on data retention and egress.
[02:52] Rahim: perf-wise, we need to define acceptance: p95 latency under 450ms for typical short inference path (<=256 tokens) and under 1s for 1k sequences, otherwise we'll need fallback to smaller model variant. is that realistic?
[03:10] Ethan: for 256 tokens p95 450ms is achievable on the dedicated NV-series we tested. for 1k sequences depends on quantization and batching strategy — we can hit ~800-1,000ms with the right KV cache warmup and 4-bit quant, but we'd like to run your trace through our benchmark harness to be sure.
[03:35] Rahim: ok we'll send a trace.
[03:38] Lina: on costs, we don't want surprises. can you show like three commitment tiers with cost per 1M tokens and dedicated hourly rates?
[03:47] Sofia: yes, we'll do three tiers: small (pilot), mid (stable traffic), large (99th percentile headroom) — include burst pricing and fallback.
[03:56] [crosstalk — someone on mute] "sorry, go ahead"
[04:02] Omar: backups — during the spike we also saw higher host CPU on our NAT gateway (probably due to many small requests) — any advice on client-side batching?
[04:14] Ethan: client-side batching helps a lot, especially for many small short prompts. but it's a balance: batching increases latency if you need immediate single-response times. we can suggest batch windows and adaptive batching with our SDK.
[04:33] Diane: okay, timeline: procurement wants a pilot to start first week of Jan. can we target a 3-week pilot: week 1 setup & security onboarding, week 2 benchmark + stress, week 3 rollout + acceptance?
[04:48] Sofia: that timeline seems reasonable. pilot acceptance criteria: 1) p95 latency targets met on representative trace, 2) error rate <0.1%, 3) cost per token within the mid-tier threshold.
[05:01] Rahim: also include quality checks — generation quality shouldn't regress when using quantized variants.
[05:08] Ethan: we'll include a small eval harness with your prompt set to check for quality regressions between fp16 and 4-bit quant.
[05:18] Lina: approvals — who signs the SOW? finance or procurement?
[05:21] Diane: procurement will handle SOW, finance will confirm budget.
[05:24] Sofia: noted. we'll loop in legal and procurement docs in the next email.
[05:30] Rahim: small ask — during benchmarking can you simulate user distribution? our traffic is 60% short prompts, 30% medium (200-600 tokens), 10% long.
[05:42] Ethan: perfect, send the distribution and a sample trace; we'll seed the harness and share results.
[05:47] [00:06:somewhere — transcription artifact: "Redwood Dedicate" vs "Dedicated"]
[05:55] Omar: about logs — retention windows matter to us. we need raw logs for 90 days and aggregated metrics for one year. do you support that?
[06:05] Maya: yes, we can configure retention tiers and export to your SIEM; extra retention is an add-on but doable.
[06:12] Lina: pricing for extra retention?
[06:15] Sofia: we'll include retention pricing as a separate line item in the proposal.
[06:19] Diane: any blockers on network egress or VPC peering with our cloud?
[06:25] Ethan: we support AWS VPC peering and private endpoints; for on-prem air-gapped it's more involved but we have an on-prem package.
[06:35] Rahim: competitors: we ran a small eval against Anthropic and found lower cost here for our workload but Anthropic had slightly better zero-shot on one prompt — we'll include that in the eval.
[06:47] Sofia: good to know — we'll add a comparison slide in the follow-up.
[06:52] Diane: great. last: rollback and canary policies — show us an example of an automatic fallback if latency crosses threshold.
[06:59] Ethan: yes, we have automatic fallback: policy based routing — if p95 crosses a set threshold we can route to a cheaper, smaller variant or to a different region; we can demo that in the pilot.
[07:12] Rahim: perfect.
[07:14] [07:20] Sofia: alright let's recap: we'll send proposal with tiered pricing, security docs, benchmark plan and sample schedule. Coraline will send traffic trace and distribution, procurement will confirm SOW owner and timeline. plan pilot kickoff for week of 12/28.
[07:33] Diane: sounds good.
[07:35] Lina: thanks, see you on the 16th when we expect the pricing.
[07:40] Sofia: thanks everyone, we'll follow up with attachments and a calendar invite for the pilot kickoff.
[07:45] [End]

Recap (auto):
- Action: Redwood to deliver pricing proposal, security pack, benchmark plan.
- Action: Customer to provide anonymized trace and SLO targets.
- Pilot kickoff targeted week of 2026-12-28.
- Acceptance: p95 latency thresholds, error rate, cost band, and quality parity checks.

title: Strategy: Launch Criteria and Fallback Matrix Sync
preview:
Meeting: Strategy: Launch Criteria and Fallback Matrix Sync
Recorded at: 2026-10-19 08:32 UTC
Duration: 78 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Lee; Maya Chen; Ethan Park; Priya Kapoor; Sofia Martinez; Liam O'Connor; Claire Nguyen; Rajesh Singh; Omar Reyes; Talia Brooks

Summary:
Quick sync to finalize the launch acceptance criteria, agree on the fallback matrix for degraded capacity scenarios, and assign owners for runbook updates. Heavy focus on canary thresholds, SLOs, and legal/priv settings for Private deployments.

Topics:
- Launch acceptance criteria (perf, quality, regression windows)
- Fallback matrix (region swap, model variant, Dedicated burst)
- Observability and tracing requirement for canary
- Security gating for Private/VPC customers
- Runbook owners and oncall handoffs

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Jordan Lee: alright, uh good morning everyone, thanks for making time. quick agenda, maya you wanna kick us off on the acceptance criteria?
[00:23] Maya Chen: yeah sure, so, uh the draft i sent last night, the high level is we need three signals green: perf (p95 under target), quality (no regression on golden prompt set), and stability (no SRE severity 1s in the canary window). also we have a regression window of 48 hours post-canary.
[01:01] Priya Kapoor: just to clarify, p95 target is for hosted and for Dedicated it's slightly higher, right? because of the batching differences. we should call out sequence length buckets.
[01:23] Ethan Park: correct, Hosted p95 target 180ms for short seq, Dedicated 220ms unless customer pins a high-precision model. also need to add a note for quantized variants — they may shift p99.
[01:45] Maya Chen: yes and add acceptance thresholds per model family, because the kernel selection is different. i put a placeholder for this.
[02:12] Sofia Martinez: from SRE side, the important bit is the circuit breaker matrix. when latency crosses X for Y minutes we need to degrade to model variant or region-failover. i sketched a matrix but it's rough.
[02:40] Jordan Lee: let's surface that, ethan you own the matrix?
[02:45] Ethan Park: i'll take it, i'll, uh, i'll fill in region weights, and what we call 'tiered fallback'. note, sometimes kv cache — uh cache — (laugh) the transcription will call it kv cash in the notes — it's the KV cache eviction behavior that matters.
[03:12] Priya Kapoor: also worth calling out, when we talk about model variants, we need to be explicit about tokenization differences and output deltas. we've had customers see hallucination shifts when switching quantization.
[03:35] Claire Nguyen: design question, will the fallback to a faster model change the UX in a way we should include in the acceptance criteria? for instance a slightly different summary format can break downstream parsers.
[03:52] Maya Chen: good call, add 'backwards-compatible output format' as a soft constraint and include a deterministic test set.
[04:15] Rajesh Singh: from privacy, any fallback that routes to a different region triggers a data residency check. we need legal signoff if region changes for Private customers.
[04:34] Omar Reyes: yes, legal will need to pre-approve allowed region-fallback mappings per account. also we need KMS key policy notes if we re-encrypt on the other region.
[04:50] Liam O'Connor: operationally, we should prefer Dedicated burst and capacity re-routing over region swapping for enterprise accounts to avoid residency issues.
[05:12] Talia Brooks: scheduling note, launch window is narrow; we need a 72-hour regression window, but also a contingency to roll back within 30m if we see SRE Sev1s. who owns rollback?
[05:35] Jordan Lee: release manager owns rollback actions, talia please own the rollback doc but SREs to validate playbook.
[06:00] Ethan Park: on the fallback matrix itself, i propose tiers: 1) model variant downgrade (quantized smaller), 2) Dedicated pool burst or spill, 3) regional failover, 4) degraded mode returning cached responses. each tier has cost and quality tradeoffs.
[06:34] Priya Kapoor: quick question — for cached responses, we need clear TTLs and semantics. we've seen customers object to stale completions. set a max TTL 15s for interactive features, longer for noninteractive.
[07:00] Maya Chen: note added. also we should run a synthetic test that asserts no stale content beyond TTL with the KV cache eviction behavior.
[07:22] Sofia Martinez: telemetry — we need traces with token-level timing, not just request-level. the tracing hooks in the runtime must include which kernel path was taken and whether continuous batching occurred. otherwise we can't tie p99 spikes to batching.
[07:52] Priya Kapoor: fine but that may be heavy. maybe sample 10% of requests during canary to get trace-level detail.
[08:05] Ethan Park: agree sampling is needed; 100% traces will cost us too much and could skew p95.
[08:24] Jordan Lee: any pushback from Legal or Privacy on sampled traces?
[08:31] Rajesh Singh: we need to redact PII and ensure traces for Private deployments stay in customer VPC or are pushed to customer-managed collectors; otherwise it's not allowed.
[08:50] Omar Reyes: add clause: for Private customers, traces stored off cluster must be encrypted with the customer's KMS, and retention shortened to 30 days unless customer requests otherwise.
[09:08] Talia Brooks: back to canary success definition, we said no Sev1s and p95 under target for two consecutive windows of 30m each?
[09:25] Maya Chen: yes, two consecutive 30m windows with stable metrics, and no functional regressions on the golden prompt set.
[09:40] Priya Kapoor: i'll run the golden set during canary and produce a diff report. note transcription may show some model names wrong, like 'Redwood Dedicate' instead of Dedicated. ignore the typos.
[09:57] Jordan Lee: haha noted.
[10:12] Ethan Park: technical deep-dive: when we trigger model variant fallback, we have to warm KV cache or the latency spike will bite us. suggestion: pre-warm KV cache using a small sampling of real traffic mirrored for 5 minutes; that should reduce cold-start tails.
[10:40] Sofia Martinez: pre-warming helps but mirror ratio must be controlled. also pre-warm for quantized kernels may require different memory layout, double-check.
[11:02] Priya Kapoor: on-canary tests: i'll collect p50/p95/p99 and token-level timings, plus a lightweight semantic-similarity score for the golden set so product can decide if quality drift is acceptable.
[11:27] Claire Nguyen: for the semantic score, we need a threshold and a conservative fallback. i'd suggest thresholds per prompt category.
[11:41] Maya Chen: ok we'll add categories.
[12:00] Jordan Lee: anything about observability dashboards? liam?
[12:07] Liam O'Connor: console needs a canary dashboard that highlights canary vs baseline and shows model variant, kernel path, and region. also a one-click rollback button that ties to release manager runbook.
[12:34] Ethan Park: one-click rollback is dangerous. should be gated with at least two approvers. maybe one-click to create rollback change but not auto-execute.
[12:48] Talia Brooks: agreed, release manager executes rollback after SRE signoff, but the console can create the rollback play and notify approvers.
[13:10] Rajesh Singh: security gating: SSO onboarding must be validated for Private customers before launch, and admin RBAC for runbooks needs to be enforced. i've seen cases where runbook edits lacked audit logs.
[13:28] Omar Reyes: legal will require audit trail retention for runbook changes for at least 1 year for some regulated customers; we should flag those accounts.
[13:45] Sofia Martinez: SRE ask: add a canary increase ramp schedule. don't go 0 to 100, ramp over 6 steps, monitor p99 and error rate at each step. if p99 increases more than 25% from baseline, stop the ramp.
[14:10] Priya Kapoor: i'll script the ramp and the gating rules.
[14:22] Maya Chen: excellent. also, can we get a quick note on cost tradeoffs? if we keep Dedicated burst for enterprise, cost will spike. sales needs a cost communication template.
[14:40] Jordan Lee: sales will need a one-pager. i'll work with maya on that.
[14:52] Ethan Park: another tiny item — model routing behavior when capacity is constrained, we need an order of preference: prefer Dedicated burst, then model variant, then region failover, last resort cached responses. document this order in the fallback matrix.
[15:12] Claire Nguyen: UX should get notified when a fallback happens so front-end can show a small banner 'quality mode engaged' rather than silently changing behavior.
[15:25] Maya Chen: good. add front-end notification as a recommended integration.
[15:40] Priya Kapoor: testing note: include a regression test that simulates fallback path and asserts downstream parsers still function.
[15:55] Ethan Park: i'll add that to the automation backlog.
[16:12] Jordan Lee: ok let's go rapid-fire through owners and due dates.
[16:20] Ethan Park: fallback matrix owner, due 10/26.
[16:24] Priya Kapoor: canary perf tests owner, due 10/23.
[16:28] Sofia Martinez: SRE playbook and circuit thresholds owner, due 10/24.
[16:32] Maya Chen: launch acceptance checklist, due 10/21.
[16:36] Talia Brooks: rollback playbook owner to finalize by 10/22.
[16:45] Jordan Lee: any blockers not previously covered?
[16:48] Rajesh Singh: region-fallback legal mapping needs input from compliance on a per-account basis; may delay certain Private deployments. i'll provide a list of sensitive accounts by Friday.
[17:05] Omar Reyes: we'll prioritize customers under active contracts for exceptions.
[17:18] Liam O'Connor: logistics: schedule capacity bakeoff; ops will provide cluster time, need to pick dates.
[17:30] Maya Chen: can we aim for week of Oct 27?
[17:35] Sofia Martinez: yes that's workable.
[17:42] Jordan Lee: wrap — to recap: owners and dates as stated; fallback matrix and canary perf tests are highest priority. next sync will be after fallback matrix draft is posted, tentatively 2026-10-27.
[18:02] Priya Kapoor: quick note, i'll post raw canary traces to internal S3 and link in the doc; traces will be redacted per rajesh's guidance.
[18:15] Rajesh Singh: i'll review and list accounts that require region restrictions.
[18:22] Jordan Lee: great. any final comments?
[18:30] Claire Nguyen: just please keep UX in the loop early if any fallback changes output format.
[18:36] Jordan Lee: will do. thanks everyone. meeting adjourned.

--- End Transcript ---

References and links mentioned: fallback-matrix-draft (internal doc link placeholder), canary-golden-set.csv (internal), runbook/rollback-playbook (internal link).

Notes on transcription: occasional mis-hearings captured (eg "KV cash" for KV cache, "Redwood Dedicate"), filler words and interruptions preserved.

title: Hartwell Labs implementation immersion kickoff
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-09-25
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 49 minutes (scheduled 09:00 PT)
Location: Zoom
Attendees (recorded):
- Maya Chen (Redwood AE)
- Ethan Park (Redwood SE)
- Priya Rao (Redwood CSM)
- Dr. Lila Hart (Hartwell Labs, CTO)
- Marco Ruiz (Infra Lead)
- Sofia Nguyen (Head of ML)
- Evan Cole (Security)
- Tara Lim (PM)
- Speaker 2 (intern, joined late)

Summary:
Quick implementation/immersion kickoff for Hartwell Labs' pilot of Redwood Private (VPC) for inference of internal therapeutics research assistant. Primary concerns: network/VPC setup, data residency, SSO/SAML, auditing/KMS, latency profile for low-latency RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) flows. Agreed next steps: runbook + VPC checklist, customer to send traffic profile, Redwood SE to propose benchmark plan and instance sizing.

Topics covered:
- Onboarding timeline and success criteria
- Network design (VPC peering, egress) and IP allowlisting
- Security: SAML SSO, KMS/HSM, SOC 2 questions
- Pilot scope: 2-week pilot, 100k tokens/day peak sim
- Tradeoffs: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (they want Private/VPC but asked about canary fallback)
- Observability: token-level cost, trace IDs, alerting

Action items (quick list presented on call):
- Redwood: runbook draft, perf plan, pilot playbook
- Hartwell: CIDR ranges, traffic examples, stakeholder contact for security review

Questions captured:
- Can we do SAML with our Okta? (yes, standard SAML flow)
- How will KV cache be handled across nodes? (Ethan: continuous batching + prefix caching, note: sometimes transcribed as KV cash)
- What are the SLAs during pilot vs production? (Priya: pilot best-effort, Dedicated has SLA)

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya: Okay folks, thanks for joining. I'm Maya from Redwood, I'm the AE on this account. Quick roll call?
[00:08] Lila: Hi, Lila here. Thank you.
[00:10] Marco: Marco — infra.
[00:12] Sofia: Sofia, ML.
[00:13] Ethan: Ethan, SE — I'll drive the perf and infra bits.
[00:16] Priya: Priya, CSM.
[00:18] Evan: Evan, security.
[00:19] Tara: Tara, PM.
[00:20] Speaker 2: (muffled) yep, here.

[00:30] Maya: Great. Quick agenda: go over pilot goals, runbook and timeline, security and VPC details, perf benchmarking, then next steps. Any additions?
[00:38] Tara: Maybe a short section on who approves go/no-go on the customer side.

[16:10] Priya: There's an add-on for extended retention; we'll include options.

title: HydraForge / Redwood Partnership - Connector Fit & Pilot Scoping
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-09-02
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~62 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Lena Ortiz (CTO - HydraForge), Samir Patel (Infra), Grace Huang (Security), Oliver King (Product)

Summary:
HydraForge is evaluating Redwood for a hybrid connector (managed + on-prem) to accelerate LLM inference for their platform. Main asks: predictable latency for customer-facing features, data residency and strong audit/KMS controls, and an initial pilot to validate cost/perf tradeoffs. Redwood AE/SE explained deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private), routing/fallbacks, and batching/KV-cache benefits. Customer requested benchmark plan, security docs (SOC2 / ISO), and a scoped pilot SOW.

Topics covered:
- Current infra & pain points (GPU mix, kubernetes + bare metal)
- Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs
- Latency SLOs and perf tuning (batching, seq len, quantization)
- Security/compliance needs (KMS, HSM, SSO/SAML, audit logs)
- Pilot scope and success criteria (p99 latency, tokens/sec, cost target)
- Next steps and responsibilities

Action items (captured):
- Ethan to run throughput/latency benchmarks and share raw logs
- Maya to send pilot SOW and Redwood Private security appendix
- Grace to share HydraForge compliance checklist

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya (Redwood): Hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick intros from our side — I'm Maya, I'm the AE, and Ethan is our solutions engineer.
[00:12] Lena (HydraForge): Hi, Lena here, CTO. we've got Samir on infra and Grace on security. Oliver's joining for product context. Appreciate you fitting us in.
[00:22] Ethan (Redwood): great to meet you all. quick agenda — we want to understand your stack, constraints, pilot goals, then map to Redwood deployment options.
[00:35] Samir (HydraForge): To kick off — we run a mix of A100s on k8s plus some older T4 fleets in cloud. most customer facing calls need sub-300ms tail latency today, but that's at low qps. as we scale we see tail spikes and it's driving up costs.
[00:57] Lena: yeah our product team cares about latency and deterministic billing. we're evaluating providers and also exploring an on-prem connector for regulated customers.
[01:12] Grace (HydraForge): from security: we need KMS integration, audit trails, SSO with enterprise IdP, and SOC2/ISO evidence. air-gapped options might be necessary for a handful of customers.
[01:30] Maya: totally. Redwood supports hosted API for quick start, Dedicated reserved pools, and Private (VPC/on-prem) which gives you KMS/HSM and offline options. we also do token-level audit and per-request tracing.
[01:46] Oliver (HydraForge): can you clarify how fallbacks work if a Private node is overloaded? we can't drop customer requests.
[01:56] Ethan: good question — we do smart routing by policy. you can prefer Private, but fallback to Dedicated or a compatible hosted variant. we also have canary + A/B rollout built-in so you can test model variants. fallback preserves prompt context but might use a quantized model; we'll surface that in trace logs.
[02:20] Samir: what's the overhead of routing — do we see additional latency when fallback happens?
[02:28] Ethan: negligible in our tests — routing adds a few ms. main variance is model variant and batch windowing. for low latency workloads we tune batching thresholds and enable prefix KV cache to avoid re-computing repeated context.
[02:45] Lena: KV cache — can you run that in a hybrid deployment? meaning cache on-prem with Redwood control plane in cloud?
[02:53] Ethan: yes, KV cache is local to the serving node, so in Private it's entirely on your infra. for Dedicated/Hosted we store cache in secured instance memory; we never spill unencrypted persistent KV to third-party storage unless you opt in.
[03:10] Grace: encryption at rest — we need KMS integration with our Azure Key Vault and an HSM option. do you support BYOK?
[03:20] Maya: yes, bring-your-own-key supported for Private and Dedicated setups. we can integrate with Azure Key Vault or common HSM providers. we'll include the precise KMS integration docs in the security pack.
[03:33] Oliver: what's your typical model quantization story? our models are ~13B and sometimes 70B. we can't afford big accuracy regressions.
[03:44] Ethan: we recommend mixed-precision or Q4_0/Q4_1 quant profiles for 13B which get 2-3x perf boost with minor quality delta. for 70B we have quant+kernel combos but we'd run evals on your prompts. Redwood Optimize can suggest quantization per workload.
[04:05] Lena: ok, success criteria — we want to validate: p99 latency < 350ms on typical 512-token prompts, throughput of X tokens/sec under burst, and per-token cost delta < 30% vs current. can you help frame a benchmark?
[04:22] Ethan: absolutely. proposed pilot: 2-week pilot with a small Private footprint on HydraForge infra (1-2 gpus for baseline), run mixed workloads, we capture p50/p95/p99, tokens/sec, and cost. we'll supply a benchmark harness and instructions.
[04:40] Samir: we need the harness to work inside our k8s environment — can you provide a docker image?
[04:47] Ethan: yes — we'll provide a containerized load generator and a prebuilt model bundle. we'll also share a sample k8s manifest for the operator.
[04:57] Grace: compliance — we need an audit log with request-level metadata retained for 180 days and tamper-evidence. we also require SSO and RBAC integration.
[05:08] Maya: audit logs and request tracing are standard in Redwood Private. retention windows are configurable. SSO/SAML + SCIM provisioning for RBAC are supported. we'll attach the audit log schema and retention options in the docs.
[05:22] Oliver: one hesitancy is vendor lock in and fallback behavior — if you route to hosted during a private outage are tokens leaving our control?
[05:33] Ethan: we try to avoid moving sensitive data. you can set policies to disallow routing off-prem for regulated tenants; in that case requests will queue or fail fast depending on policy. for non-regulated traffic, fallback to hosted is allowed.
[05:49] Lena: cost — can you give a rough expected per-token unit comparison for 13B non-quantized vs quantized?
[05:58] Maya: ballpark: hosted non-quant 13B -> $X/1k tokens; quantized Dedicated or Private can be ~40-60% lower, depending on batch size and cache hit rate. we'll include a sizing / cost worksheet.
[06:18] Samir: autoscale — we want to avoid long cold starts. how do burst and autoscale work with reserved pools?
[06:28] Ethan: Dedicated supports burst capacity and pre-warm pools. autoscale uses usage patterns and we expose hooks so you can pre-emptively allocate. cold start times depend on model size and quant; with cached weights, warmup is fast (seconds to low tens of seconds).
[06:45] Grace: can we get a list of certs and third-party audits?
[06:50] Maya: yes — SOC2 type II, ISO27001 summaries are in the security pack, plus network diagrams and subcontractor lists.
[07:02] Lena: what's the timeline for pilot? we have a product milestone in mid-October so ideally pilot in September.
[07:10] Maya: that's doable — pilot start 2nd week of September fits. we'll send SOW and a proposed timeline.
[07:20] Oliver: who will be the point of contact from Redwood for the technical onboarding?
[07:24] Maya: Ethan will lead the technical onboarding and be your primary SE contact; I'll own the commercial side.
[07:31] Samir: on benchmarks: can Redwood run both latency-first and cost-first profiles? we want to see trade-offs.
[07:40] Ethan: yes — we'll run two benchmark passes: low-latency (small batch, aggressive cache) and throughput/cost (larger batch, quant). we'll deliver a comparison sheet.
[07:52] Grace: about logs and PII — can we redact sensitive fields before logs leave our VPC?
[07:59] Ethan: absolutely, you can enable pre-ingest redaction or keep logs local. we'll include config snippets.
[08:10] Lena: ok sounds good. any partner channels we should loop in?
[08:15] Maya: we'll copy partnerships and solutions engineering as needed after the SOW.
[08:22] Oliver: one more — what's the rollback and canary process like if a model update degrades quality?
[08:30] Ethan: Redwood supports automatic regression alerts tied to prompt sets and can auto-roll back or route percentage traffic to the previous version. alerts tie back to eval metrics you define.
[08:45] Maya: recap and next steps — we'll send: SOW + pricing, Redwood Private security appendix (SOC2/ISO), benchmark harness (container + manifests), and proposed pilot dates. Ethan will run the first benchmark on HydraForge test image once we get access. Grace will provide compliance needs.
[09:03] Grace: i'll send our compliance matrix and required attestations by next week.
[09:10] Maya: perfect. any final questions?
[09:15] Lena: nothing else, thanks all. this was helpful.

text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-09-02
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~62 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Lena Ortiz (CTO - HydraForge), Samir Patel (Infra), Grace Huang (Security), Oliver King (Product)

Summary:
HydraForge is evaluating Redwood for a hybrid connector (managed + on-prem) to accelerate LLM inference for their platform. Main asks: predictable latency for customer-facing features, data residency and strong audit/KMS controls, and an initial pilot to validate cost/perf tradeoffs. Redwood AE/SE explained deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private), routing/fallbacks, and batching/KV-cache benefits. Customer requested benchmark plan, security docs (SOC2 / ISO), and a scoped pilot SOW.

Topics covered:
- Current infra & pain points (GPU mix, kubernetes + bare metal)
- Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs
- Latency SLOs and perf tuning (batching, seq len, quantization)
- Security/compliance needs (KMS, HSM, SSO/SAML, audit logs)
- Pilot scope and success criteria (p99 latency, tokens/sec, cost target)
- Next steps and responsibilities

Action items (captured):
- Ethan to run throughput/latency benchmarks and share raw logs
- Maya to send pilot SOW and Redwood Private security appendix
- Grace to share HydraForge compliance checklist

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya (Redwood): Hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick intros from our side — I'm Maya, I'm the AE, and Ethan is our solutions engineer.
[00:12] Lena (HydraForge): Hi, Lena here, CTO. we've got Samir on infra and Grace on security. Oliver's joining for product context. Appreciate you fitting us in.
[00:22] Ethan (Redwood): great to meet you all. quick agenda — we want to understand your stack, constraints, pilot goals, then map to Redwood deployment options.
[00:35] Samir (HydraForge): To kick off — we run a mix of A100s on k8s plus some older T4 fleets in cloud. most customer facing calls need sub-300ms tail latency today, but that's at low qps. as we scale we see tail spikes and it's driving up costs.
[00:57] Lena: yeah our product team cares about latency and deterministic billing. we're evaluating providers and also exploring an on-prem connector for regulated customers.
[01:12] Grace (HydraForge): from security: we need KMS integration, audit trails, SSO with enterprise IdP, and SOC2/ISO evidence. air-gapped options might be necessary for a handful of customers.
[01:30] Maya: totally. Redwood supports hosted API for quick start, Dedicated reserved pools, and Private (VPC/on-prem) which gives you KMS/HSM and offline options. we also do token-level audit and per-request tracing.
[01:46] Oliver (HydraForge): can you clarify how fallbacks work if a Private node is overloaded? we can't drop customer requests.
[01:56] Ethan: good question — we do smart routing by policy. you can prefer Private, but fallback to Dedicated or a compatible hosted variant. we also have canary + A/B rollout built-in so you can test model variants. fallback preserves prompt context but might use a quantized model; we'll surface that in trace logs.
[02:20] Samir: what's the overhead of routing — do we see additional latency when fallback happens?
[02:28] Ethan: negligible in our tests — routing adds a few ms. main variance is model variant and batch windowing. for low latency workloads we tune batching thresholds and enable prefix KV cache to avoid re-computing repeated context.
[02:45] Lena: KV cache — can you run that in a hybrid deployment? meaning cache on-prem with Redwood control plane in cloud?
[02:53] Ethan: yes, KV cache is local to the serving node, so in Private it's entirely on your infra. for Dedicated/Hosted we store cache in secured instance memory; we never spill unencrypted persistent KV to third-party storage unless you opt in.
[03:10] Grace: encryption at rest — we need KMS integration with our Azure Key Vault and an HSM option. do you support BYOK?
[03:20] Maya: yes, bring-your-own-key supported for Private and Dedicated setups. we can integrate with Azure Key Vault or common HSM providers. we'll include the precise KMS integration docs in the security pack.
[03:33] Oliver: what's your typical model quantization story? our models are ~13B and sometimes 70B. we can't afford big accuracy regressions.
[03:44] Ethan: we recommend mixed-precision or Q4_0/Q4_1 quant profiles for 13B which get 2-3x perf boost with minor quality delta. for 70B we have quant+kernel combos but we'd run evals on your prompts. Redwood Optimize can suggest quantization per workload.
[04:05] Lena: ok, success criteria — we want to validate: p99 latency < 350ms on typical 512-token prompts, throughput of X tokens/sec under burst, and per-token cost delta < 30% vs current. can you help frame a benchmark?
[04:22] Ethan: absolutely. proposed pilot: 2-week pilot with a small Private footprint on HydraForge infra (1-2 gpus for baseline), run mixed workloads, we capture p50/p95/p99, tokens/sec, and cost. we'll supply a benchmark harness and instructions.
[04:40] Samir: we need the harness to work inside our k8s environment — can you provide a docker image?
[04:47] Ethan: yes — we'll provide a containerized load generator and a prebuilt model bundle. we'll also share a sample k8s manifest for the operator.
[04:57] Grace: compliance — we need an audit log with request-level metadata retained for 180 days and tamper-evidence. we also require SSO and RBAC integration.
[05:08] Maya: audit logs and request tracing are standard in Redwood Private. retention windows are configurable. SSO/SAML + SCIM provisioning for RBAC are supported. we'll attach the audit log schema and retention options in the docs.
[05:22] Oliver: one hesitancy is vendor lock in and fallback behavior — if you route to hosted during a private outage are tokens leaving our control?
[05:33] Ethan: we try to avoid moving sensitive data. you can set policies to disallow routing off-prem for regulated tenants; in that case requests will queue or fail fast depending on policy. for non-regulated traffic, fallback to hosted is allowed.
[05:49] Lena: cost — can you give a rough expected per-token unit comparison for 13B non-quantized vs quantized?
[05:58] Maya: ballpark: hosted non-quant 13B -> $X/1k tokens; quantized Dedicated or Private can be ~40-60% lower, depending on batch size and cache hit rate. we'll include a sizing / cost worksheet.
[06:18] Samir: autoscale — we want to avoid long cold starts. how do burst and autoscale work with reserved pools?
[06:28] Ethan: Dedicated supports burst capacity and pre-warm pools. autoscale uses usage patterns and we expose hooks so you can pre-emptively allocate. cold start times depend on model size and quant; with cached weights, warmup is fast (seconds to low tens of seconds).
[06:45] Grace: can we get a list of certs and third-party audits?
[06:50] Maya: yes — SOC2 type II, ISO27001 summaries are in the security pack, plus network diagrams and subcontractor lists.
[07:02] Lena: what's the timeline for pilot? we have a product milestone in mid-October so ideally pilot in September.
[07:10] Maya: that's doable — pilot start 2nd week of September fits. we'll send SOW and a proposed timeline.
[07:20] Oliver: who will be the point of contact from Redwood for the technical onboarding?
[07:24] Maya: Ethan will lead the technical onboarding and be your primary SE contact; I'll own the commercial side.
[07:31] Samir: on benchmarks: can Redwood run both latency-first and cost-first profiles? we want to see trade-offs.
[07:40] Ethan: yes — we'll run two benchmark passes: low-latency (small batch, aggressive cache) and throughput/cost (larger batch, quant). we'll deliver a comparison sheet.
[07:52] Grace: about logs and PII — can we redact sensitive fields before logs leave our VPC?
[07:59] Ethan: absolutely, you can enable pre-ingest redaction or keep logs local. we'll include config snippets.
[08:10] Lena: ok sounds good. any partner channels we should loop in?
[08:15] Maya: we'll copy partnerships and solutions engineering as needed after the SOW.
[08:22] Oliver: one more — what's the rollback and canary process like if a model update degrades quality?
[08:30] Ethan: Redwood supports automatic regression alerts tied to prompt sets and can auto-roll back or route percentage traffic to the previous version. alerts tie back to eval metrics you define.
[08:45] Maya: recap and next steps — we'll send: SOW + pricing, Redwood Private security appendix (SOC2/ISO), benchmark harness (container + manifests), and proposed pilot dates. Ethan will run the first benchmark on HydraForge test image once we get access. Grace will provide compliance needs.
[09:03] Grace: i'll send our compliance matrix and required attestations by next week.
[09:10] Maya: perfect. any final questions?
[09:15] Lena: nothing else, thanks all. this was helpful.
[09:20] Maya: thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the docs and the SOW by Friday.

(Several minutes of side discussion about logistics and calendar invites omitted)

[60:10] End of call.

00:00:00 - Ethan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Ethan Park from Redwood, AE on the partnerships team. We also have Priya and Marcos with me. How's everyone doing?
00:00:08 - Aisha: Hi Ethan, doing well, thanks. This is Aisha from Sable Horizon. We're excited to chat, we've been looking at partner onramp options.
00:00:15 - Benji: Hey, Benji here. Quick note, we may jump in with some infra specifics — apologies in advance for the nerdy stuff.
00:00:22 - Ethan: Totally welcome. Goal for today is to understand Sable's constraints and propose an initial scope for a proof-of-concept. We'll cover deployment options — hosted API, dedicated capacity, private VPC — and then talk perf, security, and next steps.
00:00:38 - Priya: I'll mostly cover the technical surface and perf approach. Quick intro on Redwood side: our runtime supports continuous batching, KV cache and quantization profiles, and we do smart routing across model variants.
00:00:52 - Tomás: When you say KV cache, do you mean key-value cache for transformer past keys? We've seen different implementations cause memory cliffs.
00:01:01 - Priya: Yep — exactly the past key/value cache, we sometimes call it K-V cash in docs (laugh) — there are optimizations to spill and reuse across requests to reduce decode costs.
00:01:12 - Aisha: Nice. So to set context: Sable Horizon runs a managed inference layer for enterprise chatbots. We have about 120 customers in EMEA/NA, primary infra is a mix of co-lo and cloud — predominantly NVIDIA A10s and some T4s for low-priority throughput. Our product team is exploring partner onramps to help us scale without owning the entire stack.
00:01:36 - Ethan: Helpful, and timeline?
00:01:38 - Aisha: We're targeting a pilot in Q4, ideally a 6-8 week technical POC, with potential commercial rollout in Q1.
00:01:45 - Priya: Great. A few clarifying q's: 1) Is data residency a hard requirement for pilot customers or for all customers? 2) Do you want the partner to manage GPUs or prefer to bring-your-own? 3) What are your p99 latency targets and concurrency needs?
00:01:59 - Tomás: Data residency is a must for certain customers — EU only — but the pilot can be with non-regulated accounts. For management, we prefer the partner to manage GPU infra but with VPC peering to our control plane, so we can have network-level access and audit. Latency — around 200-400ms p95 for short-chat generation (<=256 tokens), p99 acceptable up to 800ms depending on load.
00:02:25 - Benji: Also important: we need KMS integration for model keys and audit logs for token access. SOC2 Type II is required for any partner.
00:02:33 - Ethan: Totally understood. We have SOC2 and ISO docs we can share; our Private deployment supports customer-managed KMS/HSM and audit trails with per-request tokens. We'll add the slides in the packet.
00:02:45 - Aisha: How about model pinning and rollouts? We do A/B tests across customers, would need control to pin model versions per tenant.
00:02:53 - Marcos: Redwood supports model versioning at the route level and rollout policies — canary, percentage-based, and automatic fallback if latency or errors spike. That should map to multi-tenant pinning.
00:03:05 - Tomás: On the infra side, what's your recommended topology for hybrid — i.e., partner-managed GPUs but private networking?
00:03:12 - Priya: Two common patterns: 1) Dedicated partner pool inside a customer VPC via a private deployment with peered subnets — full control plane separation, customer owns VPC, we deploy runtime nodes in peered VPC. 2) Hosted on Redwood side with VPC egress and strict data policies — faster to stand up but less control. Both can be combined with dedicated GPU pools and can enforce KMS usage.
00:03:37 - Benji: Are there constraints around ROI and utilization? Our CFO is picky about idle GPUs.
00:03:43 - Ethan: We have autoscaling and burst policies — you can reserve base capacity for predictable latency and allow bursting to shared pools for cost efficiency. For pilots we recommend a small dedicated pool sized for expected concurrency with autoscale to handle spikes.
00:03:58 - Aisha: Perf — do you have publicized benchmarks on A10/T4?
00:04:03 - Priya: We have perf profiles in our model catalog; they vary by model family and quant. For instance, 8-bit quant on A10s tends to give 2-3x throughput improvement vs FP16 on long sequences, but you'll want to validate on your token length distribution. We'll propose a short baseline test that mirrors your usage.
00:04:21 - Tomás: Would that baseline be on our models or open models? We run a mix of Llama-3 variants and some proprietary fine-tunes.
00:04:29 - Priya: Ideally your fine-tuned models — we can support onboarding private models into a private deployment or run them in a dedicated hosted pool with encryption at rest. For the POC we can either use an exported quantized artifact or do in-situ conversion if permitted.
00:04:45 - Benji: Encryption — can you integrate with our Hashicorp Vault KMS? We prefer not to give keys off-prem.
00:04:52 - Marcos: We support external KMS integrations; HashiVault is common. For true airgap/on-prem we have Private packages that run behind customer KMS/HSM. We'll note that for the action items.
00:05:04 - Aisha: Network egress — our compliance team insists on limited egress endpoints and allowlists. Can Redwood provide fixed egress IPs or an appliance?
00:05:12 - Ethan: Yes — for dedicated and private deployments we can provide static egress via NATs or conduit endpoints. We'll include specifics in the partner doc.
00:05:20 - Benji: Sizing question: we typically see spiky usage due to chat sessions. How does Redwood handle continuous batching without harming latency?
00:05:28 - Priya: Continuous batching groups requests in micro-batches with latency-aware thresholds. If latency SLO is low, we reduce batch window; if throughput matters, we increase. Also prefix caching / KV reuse helps reduce decode work for repeated session tokens. It's a workload-specific tuning knob.
00:05:45 - Tomás: Any concerns about model kernels and quantization compatibility across your runtime and our models? We saw one vendor where 8-bit quant changed model behavior slightly.
00:05:54 - Priya: That's a common tradeoff. Our Optimize team recommends running quality regression checks tied to prompt sets and evals — we can include a small eval harness in the POC to compare outputs between baseline (your FP16) and quantized runs.
00:06:08 - Aisha: Sounds good. Commercially, what's partner pricing look like for dedicated pools?
00:06:14 - Ethan: For partner partnerships we offer a reserved capacity pricing with committed hours and overage rates. We also have a revenue-share option for channel partners. We'll include starter pricing ranges in the packet; formal quotes come after sizing.
00:06:26 - Benji: Who do we loop in for legal and procurement?
00:06:29 - Lena: I'll be the main partner ops contact; procurement typically wants an initial SOW and SOC2 evidence.
00:06:35 - Ethan: Great — Lena, we'll send the partner packet and SOW template.
00:06:40 - [15 second pause / typing sounds]
00:06:55 - Tomás: Quick nit: if you're doing KV cache reuse across tenants, how do you ensure isolation? We can't have tokens leak between customers.
00:07:03 - Priya: We partition caches by route and tenant; isolation is enforced at runtime level, and audit logs track cache access. We'll also allow tenant-level encryption keys so the cached material is unreadable without tenant KMS.
00:07:17 - Benji: Great. For the POC, what's a minimal success metric?
00:07:21 - Ethan: For tech success: 1) integration that respects VPC peering and KMS; 2) p95/p99 latency within 20% of your current baseline under target concurrency; 3) no policy/security blockers. Business success: demonstrate cost per 1k tokens reduction or comparable unit economics with autoscale config.
00:07:38 - Aisha: That seems reasonable.
00:07:41 - Marcos: Operationally, we'll include a runbook for incident response and an observability dashboard sample — token-level cost, per-route latency, and error rates.
00:07:51 - Tomás: Observability is big for us — can we have webhook hooks for trace events?
00:07:55 - Priya: Yes we have tracing hooks and per-request metadata; webhook and cloud pub/sub integrations available.
00:08:02 - Benji: Any known limitations with mixed GPU fleets? We mix A10s and T4s and want to avoid cross-GPU performance cliffs.
00:08:09 - Priya: Two options: schedule like-for-like model variants to matching GPU tier, or use model routing to send latency-sensitive traffic to the faster A10 pool and batch lower-priority to T4s. The runtime supports model variant routing by policy.
00:08:23 - Aisha: Cool. Can you walk through a hypothetical pilot flow?
00:08:27 - Priya: Sure. Step 0: NDA + security checklist. Step 1: small dedicated pool provisioned (2-4 cards) + VPC peering. Step 2: onboard a single tenant model and run a baseline synthetic and replay test with your traces. Step 3: run quality eval with a small prompt set. Step 4: iterate on quant/profile and autoscale rules. Step 5: roll to additional tenants. That sequence usually fits a 6-8 week pilot.
00:08:53 - Tomás: For replay tests, can we provide request traces with PII redacted?
00:08:57 - Priya: Yes, and if needed we can run replay in a private deployment so data doesn't leave the peered VPC.
00:09:05 - Benji: About legal — we have export control concerns for certain models. Do you have experience with export classification?
00:09:11 - Ethan: We do; we have compliance counsel and prior customers in regulated industries. We'll surface our export and export-control posture in the packet.
00:09:19 - Aisha: Any limitations for on-prem installations? Some customers require an on-prem installer.
00:09:24 - Marcos: We offer an on-prem package for air-gapped environments, but that tends to be a longer engagement due to networking/firewall and KMS integration. We suggested starting with a peered private deployment then exploring air-gap for narrow use cases.
00:09:40 - Tomás: Do you support CloudFormation/Terraform templates for infra?
00:09:44 - Priya: Yes, we can provide Terraform modules for the VPC peering, subnets, and nodepool provisioning; that can speed onboarding.
00:09:51 - Aisha: Ok, this is all helpful. Quick objection from product: how do we avoid vendor lock-in?
00:09:57 - Ethan: We avoid lock-in by supporting exportable model artifacts, clearly documented APIs, and the ability to pin models and export inference logs for local replay. Also for Private deployments customers have full control plane access.
00:10:10 - Benji: One slightly gnarly topic: GPU drivers and CUDA versions — we have a few older stacks. Can Redwood support multiple CUDA toolchains?
00:10:18 - Priya: Our runtime has a compatibility matrix; we can support a small set of CUDA versions, and for private/on-prem we can align to your environment. We'll note this as an item for Priya to validate against your inventory.
00:10:29 - Tomás: Great. Anything else on integration?
00:10:31 - Ethan: I think we've covered the main surface. We'll follow up with the partner packet, security docs, and baseline perf plan.
00:10:37 - Aisha: Perfect. Shall we set the POC kickoff date tentatively for early September?
00:10:41 - Ethan: Yes — we'll propose week of Sept 8 for the kickoff.
00:10:45 - Marcos: And for the perf baseline, if you can share a 24hr sample trace and a list of tenant-critical prompts, that will let us tune quant and batch settings.
00:10:54 - Tomás: We'll do that — Benji will scrub traces and share.
00:10:58 - Benji: Yep, I'll get the scrubbed traces to Ethan's team.
00:11:02 - Priya: Great. Any final Qs?
00:11:05 - Lena: Just admin — who will be our legal contact?
00:11:09 - Ethan: I'll cc our contracts lead, Simone Carter, in the partner packet follow-up.
00:11:14 - Aisha: Thanks everyone. Good session.
00:11:18 - Ethan: Thanks all. We'll follow up with materials and a proposed timeline.

00:05:28 - Priya: Continuous batching groups requests in micro-batches with latency-aware thresholds. If latency SLO is low, we reduce batch window; if throughput matters, we increase. Also prefix caching / KV reuse helps reduce decode work for repeated session tokens. It's a workload-specific tuning knob.
00:05:45 - Tomás: Any concerns about model kernels and quantization compatibility across your runtime and our models? We saw one vendor where 8-bit quant changed model behavior slightly.
00:05:54 - Priya: That's a common tradeoff. Our Optimize team recommends running quality regression checks tied to prompt sets and evals — we can include a small eval harness in the POC to compare outputs between baseline (your FP16) and quantized runs.
