preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-08-12
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 44 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Summary:
- Check-in with Orchidum on upcoming renewal and request to add Dedicated capacity plus EU region/private pilot.
- Discussed latency targets, cost tradeoffs, VPC/KMS/compliance constraints, and pilot success criteria.
- Agreed next steps: Redwood to send quote and benchmark artifacts; customer to confirm procurement timeline and infra details.

Topics (auto): renewal, dedicated capacity, EU private pilot, SOC2/KMS, latency SLOs, benchmark plan

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya (AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining, appreciate you taking the time. Priya, can you kick us off with current state and what you're trying to solve this quarter?
[00:22] Priya (CTO): Yeah thanks Maya. So, quick context — we're coming up on renewal in late August, and our usage has spiked 2.4x since last quarter. We're seeing more production traffic for clinical triage, which is why we're asking about dedicated capacity and pushing a EU private pilot for data residency.
[00:45] Liam (Infra): The main ask is predictable throughput and a VPC option - we need to ensure the model runs inside our V-P-C with our KMS and audit hooks. Also curious about latency SLOs at p99 under burst.
[01:10] Jorge (Head ML): From model side we have a 6k token max prompt in some flows, so sequence length distribution matters. We also want to keep quality; quantization is fine but need evaluation run.
[01:30] Ethan (SE): Got it. Quick clarifying q: when you say dedicated, are you thinking a reserved pool sized for steady state or burst-able nodes?
[01:40] Priya: Prefer pins — reserved for steady state + a small burst window. Procurement likes predictable monthly commit for budgeting.
[01:52] Maya: Okay perfect. On Redwood side, dedicated gives you reserved GPU pools, predictable throughput and isolation. We can pair that with a private EU deployment for data residency. The tradeoffs are latency & cost: dedicated reserved nodes are cheaper per-token than hosted on heavy scale but you carry the commit.
[02:20] Anika (Procurement): For procurement, the ordering question is timing. We need 2 approvals: legal & security. Legal will want the standard terms but may ask for specific indemnities for PHI.
[02:36] Priya: We will not send PHI, but clinical notes contain sensitive metadata - want audit logs and retention controls.
[02:46] Maya: Absolutely — audit logs and retention can be configured. We'll share SOC2 and audit log examples.
[02:53] Ethan: A note on performance: for p99 latency we generally target sub-300ms on n=1 short prompts, but with 6k tokens and batching behaviour it'll change. For EU private pilot we'll run a baseline benchmark with your representative prompt set.
[03:15] Jorge: Can you explain how KV cache behaves in private deployments? We saw odd memory spikes in another vendor.
[03:27] Ethan: Sure — Redwood's runtime keeps the KV cache per model shard; we can control eviction tiers and prefix cache. In the private distro it's the same engine but network-access to K-V cache metrics is via our tracing hooks. (note: "K-V cash" sometimes transcribed).
[03:51] Liam: Networking question — will we need peering or can Redwood connect via private link? Our infra team prefers S2S VPN or cloud private link to avoid public endpoints.
[04:05] Sofia (CSM): Both options supported — EU private can be deployed into your VPC with peering or private link; we'll document the connection patterns in the runbook.
[04:18] Priya: What about KMS? We are using HashiCorp Vault + HSM for keys.
[04:24] Ethan: We support customer-managed KMS; for HSM-backed flows we have integrations and will surface the key rotation and audit. We'll follow up with the KMS integration guide.
[04:37] Anika: Security will ask for SSO/SAML and RBAC details — do you have sample SAML metadata?
[04:44] Maya: Yes, we'll share SSO/SAML integration docs and RBAC examples.
[04:49] [05:00] -- timestamp jumps --
[05:01] Priya: On the model side: is there an easy way to pin model versions during canary rollouts? We want to do a canary for the upgraded capacity so we can fail back.
[05:12] Ethan: Absolutely. Rollouts support canary and A/B testing. We can pin model variants and route a small % to the new pool, with automatic fallback to the previous model if error rates exceed threshold.
[05:28] Jorge: For the pilot, we want to measure both throughput (rps) and quality (nucleus/bleu-ish evals). Can you take a look at our eval harness?
[05:38] Maya: Yes, send over a sample eval set. Ethan will provide benchmark scripts that plug into your harness.
[05:45] Jorge: We'll share a 250-example prompt set later today.
[05:50] Anika: On billing: how granular is token accounting for dedicated vs hosted? We prefer token-level cost visibility for routes.
[05:58] Maya: Billing is token-level with per-model cost breakdown; with Dedicated you'll get allocation reports by customer route and token-level splits in the Console.
[06:10] Liam: Any SLA differences for private vs hosted? We need an uptime commitment and latency SLOs for our SLAs.
[06:18] Maya: Dedicated comes with SLA-backed uptime and latency targets we can include in the contractual addendum for your committed capacity. Private (on-prem) SLAs depend on the deployment model and can be negotiated.
[06:30] [06:45] -- brief cross talk --
[06:46] Priya: What's typical ramp timeline for a two-node Dedicated commit + EU private pilot?
[06:54] Ethan: For Dedicated provision to reserved nodes in hosted infra it's about 7-10 business days; for EU private pilot involving VPC and KMS integration expect 2-4 weeks depending on ticketing and approvals.
[07:08] Priya: That's ok. We want the pilot to run perf tests and then a short production cutoff.
[07:16] Maya: We'll propose a phased plan in the quote: 1) Dedicated reservation live, 2) EU private pilot kickoff + perf, 3) go/no-go decision in pilot week 2.
[07:28] Jorge: If the private pilot shows a 1.2x latency increase due to data residency, is there an optimization playbook?
[07:36] Ethan: Yes — options include: shorter MV caches, model quantization profiles, and region kernel selection. We'll run targeted kernel selection and batching tweaks.
[07:49] Priya: On tradeoffs: procurement wants budget certainty but security wants private; we might do dedicated hosted for now, then private if we need residency. Thoughts?
[08:00] Maya: That's common — start with dedicated hosted in the EU region (if available) to get throughput, then migrate to private if residency/audit demands require it. There are lift costs but we can minimize with staged rollouts.
[08:15] Anika: What about competitor commercial language — we saw a lower headline price from BigCloud AI but they lacked audit logs and SAML.
[08:24] Maya: Total cost of ownership matters — we'll include a comparison note and show the incremental value of observability/audit.
[08:31] [09:00] -- mid-call check --
[09:02] Priya: On data retention: how long are logs kept and can you shorten retention windows?
[09:09] Sofia: Yes, retention is configurable per-tenant; for the private pilot we can default to your security policy and shorten retention for sensitive flows.
[09:18] Liam: Are there per-route token caps to prevent runaway costs?
[09:23] Ethan: Yes, Console supports per-route quotas and token caps, plus alerts.
[09:30] Jorge: For the benchmark, can you measure 95 and 99 latency and memory usage per node while we run concurrent flows (N=50)?
[09:39] Ethan: Yes, benchmark scripts will capture p50/p95/p99, GPU utilization, and KV cache memory. We'll instrument the traces.
[09:50] [11:30] -- longer exchange on technical specifics omitted for brevity in meeting notes --
[11:35] Priya: We also need to ensure model outputs are not logged in plaintext for certain routes. Is field redaction supported?
[11:44] Maya: We have structured output features and can enable redaction at the proxy layer in private deployments. We'll detail options in runbook.
[12:00] Anika: For procurement, please include a sample SOW and a statement of work for the pilot.
[12:07] Maya: Will do.
[12:12] [15:00] -- speaking pace slows, some cross-talk --
[15:05] Priya: One more on pricing: if we commit to 12 months, can we lock prices for region add-ons?
[15:14] Maya: Yes, term commitments can lock certain price tiers; we'll include options for 12 and 24 month with discount tiers for committed usage.
[15:25] Anika: Can you include an optional 3-month break clause for compliance findings?
[15:31] Maya: We'll pass that to legal to draft language.
[15:36] [20:00] -- technical dig into canary routing and telemetry --
[20:02] Ethan: Telemetry will include per-request tracing with anonymized IDs and latency histograms; error rate thresholds trigger auto-fallback.
[20:14] Jorge: Good. For canary we want to see side-by-side delta on generation quality. Can we get diff tooling?
[20:22] Ethan: We'll surface generation diffs and token-level cost delta in the Console; also provide export CSVs for your analysis.
[20:33] Priya: For the EU private pilot, do you need any compliance paperwork pre-provisioning?
[20:38] Sofia: We'll need an onboarding questionnaire and ATO checklist filled. We'll send the forms after this call.
[20:46] [28:00] -- later in call planning next steps --
[28:05] Maya: Recap — Redwood action items: quote w/ pricing tiers and term options, perf scripts + runbook, SOC2 and SAML docs, KMS integration guide, pilot scheduling. Orchidum action items: share eval prompts, confirm approvers and procurement timing, provide VPC diagram and KMS provider.
[28:28] Priya: Sounds good. One ask: include an explicit rollback plan in the pilot docs.
[28:34] Ethan: Will add rollback steps and failover flowcharts.
[28:40] Anika: We'll aim to review the quote with legal/security by Aug 19. Procurement will try to get PO approved by Aug 25 if everything aligns.
[28:50] Maya: Great, we'll aim to hit those dates.
[29:00] Sofia: I'll send calendar invites for a pilot kickoff and a follow-up checkpoint one week into the pilot.
[29:08] Jorge: Quick follow, competitor question — if BigCloud offers a slightly lower price, how quickly can Redwood match or provide credits?
[29:20] Maya: We'll evaluate commercial concessions as part of the deal desk if competitive proof of pricing is provided.
[29:29] Priya: Ok thanks.
[29:35] [34:00] -- closing remarks --
[34:10] Maya: Final point — we'll include a small appendix in the quote outlining migration cost and estimated lift times. Expect the quote by Aug 15.
[34:20] Priya: Perfect. Thanks everyone.
[34:25] Liam: Thanks.
[34:28] Anika: Thanks all.
[34:31] Sofia: Bye.

Call end.

Attachments / references mentioned:
- Quote template (to be sent)
- SOC2 and SAML docs (link will be shared)
- KMS integration guide (link will be shared)
- Benchmark scripts & runbook (Ethan to deliver)
- Orchidum eval prompt set (customer to share)

Notes on transcription quality: medium — occasional mislabels and minor word swaps (e.g., "K-V cash" for KV cache).

title: Petrichor Systems activation - access & runway standup
preview:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2026-05-29
Start: 14:00 PDT
Duration: 58 minutes
Meeting ID: ff-2026-05-29-petrichor-activation-01
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Maya Thompson (Redwood CSM), Liam O'Reilly (Redwood SE), Isabel Martinez (CTO), Rahul Singh (Platform Eng), Nora Patel (Security Lead), Samir Rahman (PM)

Auto Summary (auto-generated, may be imperfect):
- Kickoff/standup to validate access, network, and security prerequisites for Petrichor's activation.
- Discussed VPC peering, SSO/SAML, KMS integration, data retention policy, and initial pilot scope.
- Action items assigned for runbook share, CIDR/IP allowlist, and dedicated sizing session.

Topics: access & networking, security & compliance, pilot scope, dedicated capacity sizing, next steps

Action items (also in metadata): see list above

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan Park: All right, good afternoon everyone, thanks for joining. This is Ethan from Redwood, I'm gonna hand over to Maya quick for the context.
[00:12] Maya Thompson: Hi team, thanks. Quick agenda: verify who has console access, network peering, SSO, and then timeline for pilot. We're keeping this short, uh, about 45 to 60 minutes.
[00:24] Isabel Martinez: Sounds good. We got the invite, and — just to set expectations — we're trying to get one production flow by end of June, but that's flexible depending on networking.
[00:34] Rahul Singh: Quick heads up: infra team is juggling a migration so our IP ranges are in flux, so we'll need to confirm CIDR by early next week.
[00:44] Nora Patel: From security, we need to validate SOC2 attestation, SAML metadata, and our KMS flow — we encrypt at rest with our HSM through AWS KMS.
[00:54] Ethan Park: Great, thanks Nora. We have SOC2 Type II docs in the security pack; I'll share them after this call. For KMS, Redwood supports BYOK via KMS integration and customer-provided keys for Private deployments — we can walk through the steps.
[01:08] Samir Rahman: One ask: can we do initial eval on hosted first, and then move to Dedicated if throughput looks good? We want to measure latency and token cost.
[01:20] Liam O'Reilly: Yeah, that's a common pattern. We usually recommend starting hosted for quick iteration, then switch to Dedicated for predictable throughput. We can also run a short benchmark; I'll propose parameters.

[01:35] [screen share reference: onboarding-runbook v2 - link shared in chat]
[01:40] Maya Thompson: Ethan, could you confirm who on Petrichor has console accounts? I see Samir hasn't logged in yet.
[01:46] Samir Rahman: I tried, got an invite but the link errored out — maybe SSO not configured.
[01:52] Ethan Park: Ok, Maya will re-send invite and temporary API key. Samir, try incognito if SSO cookie is weird.
[01:58] Maya Thompson: I'll resend. Also note — temporary keys expire in 7 days.

[02:05] Rahul Singh: About VPC peering — are you doing route-based peering or AWS VPC peering? We prefer VPC peering with static routes for auditability.
[02:15] Liam O'Reilly: Redwood Private supports both; for Hosted we offer an IP allowlist approach, for Private (VPC) we can do VPC peering or Transit Gateway depending on scale. There is a small NAT egress consideration for KV-cache traffic.
[02:31] Rahul Singh: NAT egress — can you elaborate? We want to ensure no keys or PII slip out.
[02:36] Liam O'Reilly: Sure — specifically, when we route to model shards for cold starts there can be egress to model infra; with Private it's internal only. We'll map flow in the network diagram.

[02:50] Nora Patel: Regarding data retention — our policy is 30 days for logs, 7 days for temp caches, and nothing stored in plaintext outside our KMS. How does Redwood handle prompt and response retention?
[03:02] Ethan Park: By default hosted retains logs for 30 days for debug; dedicated/private deployments can be configured for zero retention. We also have token-level redaction for PII if you want to enable it. I'll add links to the retention docs.

[03:18] Isabel Martinez: Do you have audit logs that show token-level usage by user id? We need per-request correlation for compliance.
[03:26] Maya Thompson: Yes, console exposes per-token cost and latency and the audit trail includes request IDs, user, model, and route. Retention can be set; export options to our SIEM are available via S3 sink.
[03:38] Nora Patel: Great — can you confirm encryption in transit? TLS is a must.
[03:42] Liam O'Reilly: All network traffic is TLS 1.2+ by default. For Private we can also use mTLS if required.

[03:52] [05:00 would have been a separate doc share — messy auto-transcript here]
[04:02] Samir Rahman: For the pilot scope, we want to exercise two flows: 1) customer support triage (low latency, lots of short requests), and 2) report summarization (batch, longer seq). We'll need guidance on batching and quantization.
[04:18] Ethan Park: Good — that maps to our Optimize recommendations: continuous batching for low cost on batch jobs, and small-batch/low-latency config for triage. We'll provide suggested batch sizes and quant profiles.
[04:30] Rahul Singh: Any guidance on model selection? We're using open models now — size tradeoffs vs latency/cost?
[04:36] Liam O'Reilly: We'll suggest a primary model family that's Redwood-verified for low-latency and a fallback cheaper quantized variant. For example, start with the 7B quant profile for latency, and fallback to 4-bit quant reuse for high cost periods.
[04:50] Nora Patel: Are there formal SLAs for hosted? Our procurement needs something concrete if we go hosted for pilot.
[04:58] Maya Thompson: Hosted has uptime targets but enterprise SLAs are part of Dedicated/reserved contracts. For pilots we can commit to monitoring and response SLAs via our CSM.
[05:08] Isabel Martinez: We're leaning toward Private if we can get the KMS integration and peering in the timeline. What's realistic?
[05:15] Ethan Park: If network approvals go quickly we can stand up a Private environment in ~2-3 weeks; dedicated capacity procurement can add lead time. We should plan for a parallel hosted fallback to avoid blocking.

[05:28] [06:00] Rahul Singh: One technical ask — encrypting request payloads with our KMS before sending would be ideal. Does Redwood support client-side envelope encryption?
[05:36] Liam O'Reilly: Not natively in the hosted flow — we rely on TLS + server-side KMS. For Private you can bring your own key and we can do envelope encryption at the gateway layer; that's an engineering project but doable. We'll raise an RFC if you want to pursue client-side.

[05:52] Samir Rahman: Can we do a short perf smoke test next week to get latency baselines? 1000 requests with 16 token prompt, 64 response.
[05:59] Liam O'Reilly: Yep, I can run that. I'll need a test corpus and target SLOs. I'll send a spreadsheet for the benchmark parameters.

[06:08] Maya Thompson: Ok let's recap next steps now so we have alignment: Ethan will share runbook and SOC2 pack; Nora will share KMS contact and CIDR; Liam will schedule the sizing call and run benchmark; Rahul will confirm CIDR next week.
[06:22] Nora Patel: Note — procurement needs pricing for a short dedicated pilot vs hosted usage estimate. Could you share sample cost calc?
[06:30] Ethan Park: Yes, we'll provide a draft cost estimate (tokens/month, expected QPS) and a short TCO comparison doc.

[06:40] [chat link: https://redwood.example.com/onboarding/petrichor-runbook v1.2]
[06:45] Isabel Martinez: Quick question on roles: who will be our day-to-day support contact during the pilot?
[06:51] Maya Thompson: I'll be primary CSM, Ethan is AE for contractual items, and Liam is the SE for technical issues. For critical incidents we'll escalate to on-call infra.
[06:59] Samir Rahman: Ok, sounds good. Also send any sample prompts or rate-limiting guidance so product can mock realistic traffic.

[07:10] Liam O'Reilly: I'll include rate-limit headers, sample request payloads, and a small harness you can run locally.
[07:16] Rahul Singh: Does Redwood support internal-only model endpoints behind SSO? We want to prevent any external dashboard access.
[07:24] Ethan Park: Yes — RBAC and SSO/SAML groups can restrict console actions; we can lock down consoles entirely for the Private deployment.
[07:32] Nora Patel: We'll send SAML metadata and our IdP contact.

[07:40] Maya Thompson: Any blockers from Petrichor aside from CIDR and KMS contact?
[07:45] Isabel Martinez: Nothing major; hope to get CIDR by Monday. If that slips we'll default to hosted pilot.
[07:52] Ethan Park: Great — last thing, I'll follow up with an email summarizing actions and attach the security pack.
[08:00] [wrap-up]

Auto-generated Questions (may be incomplete):
- Timeline for Private vs Hosted pilot?
- Where to upload SAML metadata?
- Who runs the perf benchmark and what are success metrics?

Manual recap (Ethan):
- Send onboarding runbook + SOC2 docs (Ethan) by 2026-06-01
- Petrichor to share VPC CIDR and KMS contact (Nora/Rahul) by 2026-06-04
- SE session to size Dedicated and run perf smoke test (Liam) w/ proposal by 2026-06-10
- Cost estimate and TCO draft (Ethan) by 2026-06-03

Links & References mentioned in call: [onboarding runbook link: https://redwood.example.com/onboarding/petrichor-runbook] [security pack: https://redwood.example.com/security/packs/soc2_type2.pdf]

End of transcript.

title: LatticeCart - Platform & Security Onboarding Kickoff
preview:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2026-05-24
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 47 minutes (scheduled)
Location: Zoom
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Maya Singh (Redwood SE), Jordan Lee (Redwood CSM), Rachel Kim (LatticeCart Head of ML), Carlos Mendes (LatticeCart Platform Eng), Priya Nair (LatticeCart Sec), Oliver Grant (Product Ops)

Summary: Quick intro to onboard LatticeCart onto Redwood's hosted + private path; discuss network/topology, security checklist (KMS/retention), pilot scope (search rerank + chat ops), success metrics (99th p99 latency, cost target), and next steps for pilot start.

[00:00] Ethan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Uh quick roll call — Ethan here, I'm the AE, with Maya our SE and Jordan CSM.
[00:17] Rachel: Hi, Rachel — thanks, yeah excited to get this kicked off.
[00:22] Carlos: Carlos here.
[00:25] Priya: Priya on security.
[00:28] Oliver: Oliver — product ops.
[00:30] Jordan: Great, thanks all. Quick agenda: network and access, security and compliance, pilot scope & success criteria, timeline. We'll keep this tight.

[01:02] Maya: I can start with a high level of how we usually run onboarding. Two common patterns: hosted API first for feature validation, then we either expand to Dedicated or Private depending on isolation/regulatory needs. For teams with strict data residency or VPC requirements we do a Private deployment with a customer managed control plane, usually involves VPC peering or transit gateway and a handful of firewall rules.
[01:28] Rachel: For us — we want quick results but we need to keep PII inside our network. So likely hosted but with prefix hashing? uh no, we actually want Redwood Private on our VPC if the network lift is reasonable.
[01:44] Ethan: Totally reasonable. We'll cover both options. For pilot, many customers do a hybrid: initial PoC on Hosted with obfuscated payloads then move to Private for production.

[02:05] Priya: On security, main asks: SOC2 compliance docs, keys management — we use AWS KMS with customer CMKs — and audit logs. Also SAML SSO and RBAC for our users.
[02:21] Ethan: We have SOC2 and ISO docs; I'll share those. KMS is supported — for Private deployments we can integrate your KMS via an IAM role or external HSM in certain cases. We do token-level audit logs and can push logs to your S3 or SIEM.

[02:48] Carlos: Network-wise we're on AWS us-east-1. We'll want peering/tgw. What ports and endpoints do you need?
[02:56] Maya: For Private: you'll need to allow outbound to our control plane on 443 (redacted endpoint), and internal traffic within the VPC for model serving nodes — typically we give you a small set of private subnets to route to. For hosted, it's simpler — just HTTPS to the API, with IP allowlisting as needed. Note: latency expectations differ — hosted runs in our shared pool, Private is dedicated and lower jitter.

[03:25] Rachel: Performance is key — our product does live search rerank sub-100ms p95 for sessions. What's realistic?
[03:31] Maya: For shorter context and standard Llama-like 2B to 7B models you'll see p95 around 60-120ms on Dedicated GPUs depending on batching. p99 will be higher. We'll include sample benchmarks.
[03:44] Speaker 4: uh we also have caching strategies — prefix and KV cache — that help for repeated Qs.
[03:52] Oliver: On the metrics side, we care about tail latency and token cost per 1m queries.

[04:12] Maya: Right. For cost we look at three levers: batching, quantization profile, and routing policy. We'll propose a config that keeps cost within your target while preserving rerank quality.

[04:29] Rachel: Questions on data — we send product catalog and user context. Any special considerations for embeddings? We plan to embed product descriptions and store them on our side.
[04:38] Ethan: Embeddings are stateless; typical advice is to store embeddings on your side and only call our embedding endpoint. If you're embedding sensitive fields, you can use Private and KMS.

[05:01] Priya: We need to confirm data deletion policies — logs, prompt storage, and whether you keep KV cache.
[05:07] Ethan: By default hosted retains transient logs for 30 days for diagnostics, but for enterprise we can set retention or opt-out. Private gives you full control — you can disable persistence or set retention in your policy.

[05:30] Carlos: Can we run small perf tests on our own hardware? We want to baseline.
[05:35] Maya: Yes. We provide a benchmark harness — you'll run a short suite against sample models. We'll also run concurrent runs on Redwood Dedicated nodes to compare. I'll send the harness and instructions.

[06:02] Oliver: Timeline — we'd like pilot in two weeks if possible. What does your usual provisioning look like?
[06:08] Jordan: For Hosted pilot we can start within days; for Private provisioning with peering typically 7-14 business days for network setup + security review. Since you want two weeks, we could start hosted with synthetic data and plan Private cutover after the initial validation.

[06:28] Rachel: That works. Key stakeholders: I'll lead ML evaluation; Carlos will do infra; Priya will sign off on security; Oliver will track product KPIs.

[06:42] Ethan: Great. Now quick deep dive on expected logs and SSO.
[06:47] Priya: SSO must be SAML 2.0; we use Okta. Can we provision RBAC with groups mapped to Prod/Stage?
[06:55] Maya: Yes, we map SAML group attributes to roles in the Console. For audit logs we can stream to your SIEM through Kinesis or push to S3. We'll include the mapping doc.

[07:12] Rachel: About KMS — we need a flow diagram for how keys are used.
[07:16] Ethan: I'll send the KMS integration doc. For Private, you can supply a key policy and we use an IAM role to encrypt/decrypt model artifacts and customer caches. Hosted has an abstracted key layer but you can request CMEK options on enterprise plans.

[07:41] Speaker 2: there's also a mention of tokenization and PII — we can add an upfront scrubbing pass client-side.

[07:51] Priya: Yes we will scrub PII before calling the API; still we want assurances around transient traces in service logs.
[07:58] Ethan: We'll include the exact fields kept in logs. By default we redact detected obvious PII but it's not perfect; with Private you can turn logging off.

[08:18] Oliver: For the pilot scope let's be concrete: 1) Search rerank endpoint (batching requests) and 2) Internal support chat assistant (short context, function calls).
[08:29] Maya: Good concise scope. For rerank we'll provide a small eval harness and also a randomized sample set for quality regressions. Function calls for chat — we support structured JSON outputs.

[08:50] Rachel: Success criteria: p95 latency <= 120ms for rerank at 50qps baseline, quality (NDCG@10 delta < 0.02 vs current model), and cost < $X per 100k queries — we'll define $X in follow-up.

[09:05] Ethan: Sounds good. We'll draft a pilot plan including the perf runbook and cost estimate.

[09:20] Carlos: On infra access — who on your side will touch our VPC? We need an ops contact.
[09:26] Ethan: I'll be primary contact; Maya will handle the technical runway and our infra eng Samir will be backup — I'll include Samir on the runbook email.

[09:42] Jordan: Okay next, blockers: Priya anything blocking timeline from security perspective?
[09:48] Priya: We need the SOC2 and encryption docs and to confirm no plaintext storage of sensitive attributes. Also we will run a brief pen test or at least a network scan post-peering.
[09:58] Ethan: Understood. I'll send SOC2 and the security FAQ now and note the pen-test step for the cutover checklist.

[10:12] Maya: One more note on monitoring — we'll expose token-level cost and latency views in Console. For Private, you can forward metrics to Prometheus/Grafana.
[10:24] Oliver: Great — we need alerts for p99 latency spikes and error rates.
[10:29] Maya: We'll include recommended alert thresholds in the runbook.

[10:38] Rachel: On data residency, we operate across us-east and eu-west. Initially we'll stay us-east. Will Redwood support multi-region later?
[10:45] Maya: Yes, we support region routing policies and can pin model variants to regions; we'll do phased expansion after pilot.

[11:00] Speaker 1: small sidenote — competitor question?

text:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2026-05-24
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 47 minutes (scheduled)
Location: Zoom
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Maya Singh (Redwood SE), Jordan Lee (Redwood CSM), Rachel Kim (LatticeCart Head of ML), Carlos Mendes (LatticeCart Platform Eng), Priya Nair (LatticeCart Sec), Oliver Grant (Product Ops)

Summary: Quick intro to onboard LatticeCart onto Redwood's hosted + private path; discuss network/topology, security checklist (KMS/retention), pilot scope (search rerank + chat ops), success metrics (99th p99 latency, cost target), and next steps for pilot start.

[00:00] Ethan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Uh quick roll call — Ethan here, I'm the AE, with Maya our SE and Jordan CSM.
[00:17] Rachel: Hi, Rachel — thanks, yeah excited to get this kicked off.
[00:22] Carlos: Carlos here.
[00:25] Priya: Priya on security.
[00:28] Oliver: Oliver — product ops.
[00:30] Jordan: Great, thanks all. Quick agenda: network and access, security and compliance, pilot scope & success criteria, timeline. We'll keep this tight.

[01:02] Maya: I can start with a high level of how we usually run onboarding. Two common patterns: hosted API first for feature validation, then we either expand to Dedicated or Private depending on isolation/regulatory needs. For teams with strict data residency or VPC requirements we do a Private deployment with a customer managed control plane, usually involves VPC peering or transit gateway and a handful of firewall rules.
[01:28] Rachel: For us — we want quick results but we need to keep PII inside our network. So likely hosted but with prefix hashing? uh no, we actually want Redwood Private on our VPC if the network lift is reasonable.
[01:44] Ethan: Totally reasonable. We'll cover both options. For pilot, many customers do a hybrid: initial PoC on Hosted with obfuscated payloads then move to Private for production.

[02:05] Priya: On security, main asks: SOC2 compliance docs, keys management — we use AWS KMS with customer CMKs — and audit logs. Also SAML SSO and RBAC for our users.
[02:21] Ethan: We have SOC2 and ISO docs; I'll share those. KMS is supported — for Private deployments we can integrate your KMS via an IAM role or external HSM in certain cases. We do token-level audit logs and can push logs to your S3 or SIEM.

[02:48] Carlos: Network-wise we're on AWS us-east-1. We'll want peering/tgw. What ports and endpoints do you need?
[02:56] Maya: For Private: you'll need to allow outbound to our control plane on 443 (redacted endpoint), and internal traffic within the VPC for model serving nodes — typically we give you a small set of private subnets to route to. For hosted, it's simpler — just HTTPS to the API, with IP allowlisting as needed. Note: latency expectations differ — hosted runs in our shared pool, Private is dedicated and lower jitter.

[03:25] Rachel: Performance is key — our product does live search rerank sub-100ms p95 for sessions. What's realistic?
[03:31] Maya: For shorter context and standard Llama-like 2B to 7B models you'll see p95 around 60-120ms on Dedicated GPUs depending on batching. p99 will be higher. We'll include sample benchmarks.
[03:44] Speaker 4: uh we also have caching strategies — prefix and KV cache — that help for repeated Qs.
[03:52] Oliver: On the metrics side, we care about tail latency and token cost per 1m queries.

[04:12] Maya: Right. For cost we look at three levers: batching, quantization profile, and routing policy. We'll propose a config that keeps cost within your target while preserving rerank quality.

[04:29] Rachel: Questions on data — we send product catalog and user context. Any special considerations for embeddings? We plan to embed product descriptions and store them on our side.
[04:38] Ethan: Embeddings are stateless; typical advice is to store embeddings on your side and only call our embedding endpoint. If you're embedding sensitive fields, you can use Private and KMS.

[05:01] Priya: We need to confirm data deletion policies — logs, prompt storage, and whether you keep KV cache.
[05:07] Ethan: By default hosted retains transient logs for 30 days for diagnostics, but for enterprise we can set retention or opt-out. Private gives you full control — you can disable persistence or set retention in your policy.

[05:30] Carlos: Can we run small perf tests on our own hardware? We want to baseline.
[05:35] Maya: Yes. We provide a benchmark harness — you'll run a short suite against sample models. We'll also run concurrent runs on Redwood Dedicated nodes to compare. I'll send the harness and instructions.

[06:02] Oliver: Timeline — we'd like pilot in two weeks if possible. What does your usual provisioning look like?
[06:08] Jordan: For Hosted pilot we can start within days; for Private provisioning with peering typically 7-14 business days for network setup + security review. Since you want two weeks, we could start hosted with synthetic data and plan Private cutover after the initial validation.

[06:28] Rachel: That works. Key stakeholders: I'll lead ML evaluation; Carlos will do infra; Priya will sign off on security; Oliver will track product KPIs.

[06:42] Ethan: Great. Now quick deep dive on expected logs and SSO.
[06:47] Priya: SSO must be SAML 2.0; we use Okta. Can we provision RBAC with groups mapped to Prod/Stage?
[06:55] Maya: Yes, we map SAML group attributes to roles in the Console. For audit logs we can stream to your SIEM through Kinesis or push to S3. We'll include the mapping doc.

[07:12] Rachel: About KMS — we need a flow diagram for how keys are used.
[07:16] Ethan: I'll send the KMS integration doc. For Private, you can supply a key policy and we use an IAM role to encrypt/decrypt model artifacts and customer caches. Hosted has an abstracted key layer but you can request CMEK options on enterprise plans.

[07:41] Speaker 2: there's also a mention of tokenization and PII — we can add an upfront scrubbing pass client-side.

[07:51] Priya: Yes we will scrub PII before calling the API; still we want assurances around transient traces in service logs.
[07:58] Ethan: We'll include the exact fields kept in logs. By default we redact detected obvious PII but it's not perfect; with Private you can turn logging off.

[08:18] Oliver: For the pilot scope let's be concrete: 1) Search rerank endpoint (batching requests) and 2) Internal support chat assistant (short context, function calls).
[08:29] Maya: Good concise scope. For rerank we'll provide a small eval harness and also a randomized sample set for quality regressions. Function calls for chat — we support structured JSON outputs.

[08:50] Rachel: Success criteria: p95 latency <= 120ms for rerank at 50qps baseline, quality (NDCG@10 delta < 0.02 vs current model), and cost < $X per 100k queries — we'll define $X in follow-up.

[09:05] Ethan: Sounds good. We'll draft a pilot plan including the perf runbook and cost estimate.

[09:20] Carlos: On infra access — who on your side will touch our VPC? We need an ops contact.
[09:26] Ethan: I'll be primary contact; Maya will handle the technical runway and our infra eng Samir will be backup — I'll include Samir on the runbook email.

[09:42] Jordan: Okay next, blockers: Priya anything blocking timeline from security perspective?
[09:48] Priya: We need the SOC2 and encryption docs and to confirm no plaintext storage of sensitive attributes. Also we will run a brief pen test or at least a network scan post-peering.
[09:58] Ethan: Understood. I'll send SOC2 and the security FAQ now and note the pen-test step for the cutover checklist.

[10:12] Maya: One more note on monitoring — we'll expose token-level cost and latency views in Console. For Private, you can forward metrics to Prometheus/Grafana.
[10:24] Oliver: Great — we need alerts for p99 latency spikes and error rates.
[10:29] Maya: We'll include recommended alert thresholds in the runbook.

[10:38] Rachel: On data residency, we operate across us-east and eu-west. Initially we'll stay us-east. Will Redwood support multi-region later?
[10:45] Maya: Yes, we support region routing policies and can pin model variants to regions; we'll do phased expansion after pilot.

[11:00] Speaker 1: small sidenote — competitor question?
[11:03] Oliver: We evaluated self-hosting internally; main reason to switch would be runtime ops savings. Need to see cost vs ops tradeoff.
[11:12] Ethan: We'll include a comparison sheet as an appendix to the pilot plan showing unit economics (ops hours vs infra + Redwood fees).

[11:30] Rachel: Ok wrap — can we summarize actions and dates?
[11:35] Jordan: Sure. Ethan to send runbook, SOC2, KMS doc by 5/27. Maya to send benchmark harness by 5/29. Carlos to share CIDR and peering contact by 5/28. Priya to confirm retention policy by 5/31. Jordan to send pilot calendar invite for week of 6/8.

[11:58] Ethan: Thanks everyone, we'll follow up in email. If anything urgent ping me.
[12:04] Rachel: Thanks all.

End of Transcript.

title: Containment Handoff & Systems Hardening Plan - HorizonPay
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-22
Start time: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~73 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Marisol Chen (Redwood SE), Liam O'Connor (Redwood CSM), Dana Rivera (HorizonPay VP Eng), Tomás Alvarez (HorizonPay SRE), Priya Menon (Product), Olivia Zhang (Security)

Summary:
Quick containment retrospective after Friday's service degradation; handoff from on-call to remediation + long-term hardening plan. Focus on KV caching behavior, routing fallback, observability gaps, and security/compliance signoffs for any dedicated or private deployment.

Topics:
- Brief timeline of incident and immediate mitigations
- Root-cause hypotheses and evidence gaps (KV cache warmup, autoscaler ripple, blob-storage cold reads)
- Customer security/compliance constraints for Dedicated/Private fixes
- Roadmap: short-term runbook updates, medium-term canary + fallback validation, long-term infra hardening

Action items (recorded in metadata as well): see action_items list

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan Park: Hey everybody, thanks for joining. We wanted to run through the handoff from the on-call postmortem, make sure the containment is stable and set a clear remediation path. This'll be fairly action-oriented.
[00:18] Dana Rivera: Thanks. Quick note — our execs want a timeline for customer impact and what prevents recurrence. We already told them "we're doing a deep dive," but need specifics.
[00:30] Marisol Chen: Understood. I'll start with a short timeline from Redwood's perspective and then Tomás you can add the SRE view.
[00:35] Marisol Chen: Friday 03:12 UTC we saw tail latency climb, at 03:18 auto-scaler triggered adding nodes, at ~03:22 a KV cache flush happened during a rolling maintenance on the primary pool which caused a large number of cold-starts. Around 03:28 we observed backup routing to the second region which had quant/planning differences and that introduced higher token compute overhead.
[01:05] Tomás Alvarez: Yeah, on our end we saw the KV cash — sorry cash — cache miss storm. It coincided with a deployment in our control plane where a small config change bumped max inflight to 512. That had downstream effects on batching and queue lengths.
[01:35] Ethan Park: That's helpful. We think the loop was: cache miss spike -> higher request latency -> autoscaler scaled -> new nodes had empty KV sets -> some requests hit model variants with larger context which increased per-token compute -> regional fallback kept sending to cheaper model variant leading to throughput collapse.
[02:10] Priya Menon: Can you clarify "model variants"? Are we talking quantized vs float or different sequence-length-optimized checkpoints?
[02:25] Marisol Chen: Slightly messy there — Redwood chooses fallback variants by latency/cost policy. In this case the fallback selected a larger unquantized variant in the secondary region because the smaller one wasn't available, so per-token time went up. Our logs label the variants but some labels were mangled in the export, so we need to attach the sanitized mapping.
[03:05] Olivia Zhang: From security, our execs want assurance that any log-sharing excludes PII and that the private deployment work won't require exfil of keys. KMS/HSM workflow questions next.
[03:28] Liam O'Connor: Noted — we'll provide a sanitized slice and a checklist for KMS integration. Ethan will own the sample export.
[04:12] Dana Rivera: What's the immediate mitigation we put in place Friday? Did we revert the config?
[04:20] Tomás Alvarez: We rolled back the control-plane config at 03:40, re-warmed the caches using a synthetic fanout job, and pinned traffic away from the secondary region for about 40 minutes. That stabilized tail latency but the team saw residual token cost increases for hours.
[04:55] Marisol Chen: Also Redwood toggled aggressive prefix caching and disabled one of the fallbacks to stop variant switching. That bought time but it's not a long-term fix.
[05:18] Ethan Park: Right, so short-term we have containment. Medium-term we need: a canary for config changes that affect inflight, a pre-warming strategy for KV cache on scale-out, and improved routing policies so secondary region fallback doesn't choose higher-latency variants.
[05:48] Priya Menon: Pre-warming sounds expensive. We are cost-conscious. Can you estimate the overhead?
[05:55] Marisol Chen: There's a tradeoff. A targeted pre-warm that only fetches hot prefixes and uses a low-priority background job adds minimal delta. Full warm is pricey. Redwood Optimize can analyze access patterns; we'll include a cost delta estimate.
[06:28] Dana Rivera: We also need a timeline and owners. Who will shepherd the canary?
[06:34] Ethan Park: Marisol and Tomás should pair on the canary config. We'll help draft the checklist and run the first rehearsal. Marisol, propose a time next week.
[07:02] Tomás Alvarez: We can run the rehearsal in staging, mirror traffic at 10% for the first run, then step up. We need a rollback window documented.
[07:20] Marisol Chen: Agreed. We'll produce step-by-step with thresholds.
[07:40] Olivia Zhang: For Dedicated or Private options, our compliance team requires explicit KMS integration and audit logging retention of 12 months. Also, data residency constraints — we can't route EU traffic to non-EU regions.
[08:05] Ethan Park: That aligns with Redwood Private's VPC deployment model; we have an on-prem package and KMS hooks. We'll include a one-page compliance mapping.
[08:30] Dana Rivera: Can you confirm if the Dedicated product supports per-tenant KMS or it's per-cluster? We need per-tenant if multiple business units share infra.
[08:40] Marisol Chen: Currently per-cluster KMS is standard but per-tenant is available with additional configuration; we'll capture the options in the document.
[09:05] Priya Menon: There's also a telemetry gap — our dashboards didn't show cache miss ratio down to the prefix level quickly enough. Is that on Redwood's side?
[09:18] Marisol Chen: Partially. We ingest a lot of telemetry; default retention and aggregation can hide prefix-level spikes. We can add a short-term alert to surface high prefix-miss ratios and increase retention for the incident window.
[09:45] Tomás Alvarez: If we increase retention, we need to check storage cost. We'll coordinate cost budgets.
[10:02] Ethan Park: On the postmortem, we should set clear indicators of recurrence: repeated cache-miss storms after scale events, variant switching frequency > X per minute, and unexpected autoscaler behavior. We'll propose thresholds.
[10:28] Dana Rivera: Restore confidence is important for execs. Can we do a tabletop sim that shows the runbook in action?
[10:35] Liam O'Connor: Yes, tabletop is a good idea. We can schedule a 60-minute rehearsal and invite stakeholders; practice the rollback and the communications.
[11:00] Marisol Chen: I'll draft the runbook and the rehearsal script.
[11:15] Ethan Park: A quick question about the deployment that triggered this — Tomás, you said max inflight bumped to 512. Was that an intended change?
[11:22] Tomás Alvarez: It was intended but the setting was incompatible with our batching assumptions; basically the effective concurrency per worker changed which interacted badly with our evictions.
[11:40] Priya Menon: We should put a policy in place that any inflight/concurrency change goes through a safety checklist.
[11:48] Marisol Chen: Redwood recommends a simple canary and a kill-switch via routing policy. We'll add 'inflight changes require canary' to the checklist.
[12:10] Olivia Zhang: Security asks: will the sanitized logs include any session tokens or customer IDs? We need guarantees.
[12:20] Ethan Park: We'll filter tokens; logs will be hashed/obfuscated. We'll provide a redaction script and the sample before sharing.
[12:40] Dana Rivera: Good. Executive asks also wanted an estimate of customer-impacted percentage. Can we approximate?
[12:48] Tomás Alvarez: From APM traces, about 2.6% of requests showed >2x tail latency during the 18-minute window. We need to validate with edge logs but it's small-ish relative to total traffic.
[13:05] Marisol Chen: We'll put that number in the postmortem but mark it as preliminary until Ethan uploads the sanitized samples.
[13:28] Priya Menon: On the fix, is a code change required in our app to avoid hitting cold prefixes?
[13:36] Tomás Alvarez: Not necessarily; better caching priming and slightly different prefetch heuristics will help. Application changes are optional.
[13:58] Ethan Park: Going forward, recommended mitigations: (1) Pre-warm hot prefixes on scale-out using low-priority background jobs; (2) Canary every control-plane config change that touches concurrency; (3) Improve routing policy to prefer latency-stable smaller variants over cost-different big variants; (4) Add telemetry alerting for prefix-miss spikes.
[14:32] Dana Rivera: Sounds good. On ownership, we'll assign Tomás to the SRE-side tasks, Priya to product acceptance criteria, and Olivia to security signoff.
[14:48] Priya Menon: Can we get a rough cost delta for pre-warm? We need that before exec ask.
[14:54] Marisol Chen: I'll include a conservative cost estimate and a best-effort optimized version where only top-100 prefixes are primed.
[15:20] Ethan Park: Okay let's walk through the specific deliverables and dates.
[15:30] Marisol Chen: Deliverable 1 — Sanitized log slice and mapping of variant labels (Ethan) — due 2026-04-24.
[15:45] Ethan Park: Confirmed.
[16:02] Marisol Chen: Deliverable 2 — Canery runbook draft (Redwood + HorizonPay) — due 2026-04-27.
[16:15] Tomás Alvarez: On the canary we'll request a 10% mirrored traffic window in staging for the first run.
[16:32] Marisol Chen: Deliverable 3 — Compliance mapping for Private/Dedicated (KMS/HSM, retention) — Ethan/Liam to draft, Olivia to review — due 2026-04-29.
[17:00] Dana Rivera: We also need a postmortem write-up for our execs — a one-page summary and a longer doc. Who owns that?
[17:08] Liam O'Connor: Redwood can provide a templated postmortem; HorizonPay will flesh in their internal impact numbers. Liam will coordinate with Dana. Due 2026-04-26.
[17:38] Priya Menon: Small note — can we include a quick section in the runbook about communications (who emails customers, what to say)? Execs asked for messaging.
[17:50] Ethan Park: We'll add a communications play and a log of who to notify at each severity level.
[18:05] Tomás Alvarez: On instrumentation, adding prefix-level retention for 48 hours around the rollout sounds good. We'll check cost.
[18:28] Marisol Chen: We'll attach a small script to export top-100 prefix hit rates daily.
[18:48] Dana Rivera: Any blockers to starting these items this week?
[18:55] Tomás Alvarez: We need the sanitized logs to validate a few hypotheses. Once we have those, we can tune prefix selection.
[19:10] Marisol Chen: Action: Ethan will upload sanitized logs to the shared S3 with read-only links.
[19:22] Ethan Park: I'll do that by Friday morning US PST — which is 2026-04-24.
[19:40] Olivia Zhang: For the Dedicated/KMS path, we'll want to do a security review once we have the compliance mapping. Expect a two-week turnaround from our side.
[19:56] Liam O'Connor: We'll mark that in the timeline.
[20:12] Priya Menon: A question about fallback policies — is it possible to avoid automatically selecting a different variant when region fallback triggers, and instead fallback to a queued policy that keeps variant consistent?
[20:27] Marisol Chen: Yes, routing can be tightened to prefer variant stickiness per customer session. We'll include that config as an option.
[20:50] Tomás Alvarez: That would reduce unexpected per-request latency spikes.
[21:05] Dana Rivera: Ok, let's close with the recap and owners.

Recap:
- Containment done Friday; residual cost and tail behavior under investigation.
- Short-term deliverables: sanitized logs (Ethan), canary runbook (Marisol+Tomás), compliance mapping (Ethan+Liam+Olivia), postmortem draft (Liam+Dana).
- Medium-term: canary rehearsal and pre-warm strategy evaluation.
- Long-term: changes to routing policies and stronger canary guardrails for control-plane changes.

Explicit next steps (from the call):
- Ethan to upload sanitized logs and variant-map (due 2026-04-24).
- Marisol to share canary/rehearsal checklist and propose rehearsal times (due 2026-04-27).
- Ethan/Liam to share compliance mapping for KMS/HSM and retention options (due 2026-04-29).
- Tomás to provide exact cache behavior metrics and config diffs (due 2026-04-26).

Closing remarks:
[22:05] Ethan Park: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the doc set and propose rehearsal times. If anyone has urgent questions ping me or Liam.
[22:20] Dana Rivera: Thanks, appreciate the fast turnaround.

End meeting.

[11:40] Priya Menon: We should put a policy in place that any inflight/concurrency change goes through a safety checklist.

title: Cobalt HealthTech listing + on-prem connector compatibility workshop
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-04-03 14:00 PDT
Duration: ~72 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Sofia Martinez (Redwood SE), Noah Li (Redwood SE), Dr. Maya Rao (Cobalt CTO), Liam Patel (Platform), Priya Singh (Security), Jordan Ellis (Product)
Summary: technical alignment for marketplace listing and on-prem connector compatibility. Focus on VPC networking, KMS/HSM key wraps, installer packaging, and gating items for HealthTech compliance.

[00:00] Ethan: hey everyone, thanks for carving time, um this is Ethan from Redwood, i'm the AE on the account. Quick roll call, we got Sofia and Noah from the eng side, and from Cobalt Maya, Liam, Priya and Jordan. want to make sure we captured anything special in the agenda?
[00:18] Dr. Maya Rao: yeah thanks Ethan, we want to validate that the marketplace listing will work with our on-prem connector and that we can deploy in our air-gapped clinical infra. also need to understand audit log behavior for SOC two and how KMS integrates.
[00:32] Sofia: great, we'll walk through the listing artifacts, the connector installer options, and then dive into authen—auth and keys. i'll also surface any known limitations like initial provisioning latency for large models.
[00:45] Liam: one quick note, our infra uses a private PKI and we have HSM-backed keys, so if there's any assumption of cloud KMS we need to highlight alternatives.
[00:59] Noah: noted. for background: Redwood supports private deployment with a customer-managed control plane; we provide an on-prem bundle and a connector that can work with VPC peering or vpn tunneling. sometimes transcript or docs call it the 'Redwood Private plane' which is the private control plane.
[01:15] Jordan: can you say a bit on packaging—like is it a dockerized installer or an opaque VM?
[01:22] Sofia: it's a container-first package, with an optional OVA for air-gapped installs. the installer includes a small agent that manages model artifacts and the local runtime. for marketplace listing (when you want to list in cloud marketplace), we also provide a reference adapter so procurement can bill via marketplace subscriptions.
[01:36] Priya: billing via marketplace is okay, but we need to ensure no customer PII leaves the premises. will the adapter phone home or call a control plane for metering?
[01:48] Ethan: good q. metering events are aggregated; for Private we can do local metering and ship only usage aggregates if you opt in. default for on-prem is to not send raw request payloads. i'll add that to the security pack.
[02:02] Dr. Maya Rao: and what about audit logs? we need immutability guarantees and integration with our SIEM, Splunk.
[02:11] Sofia: audit logs are pluggable. the agent can forward to syslog or to an S3-compatible endpoint; for immutability we recommend using object-locking in your S3 or forwarding to a write-once store in your HSM-protected pipeline. note: some customers reported the 'audit-log' header gets mangled in transcription—i mean in exports—so when you ingest, double-check schema.
[02:35] Liam: ok. on networking—how is the connector established? port requirements?
[02:41] Noah: the connector can initiate an outbound TLS tunnel to the control plane, or you can run it in peel-off mode where the control plane is inside the VPC. for marketplace-listed versions we often recommend the outbound tunnel because it simplifies firewall rules. we have a doc called 'connector-port-matrix' that'll list 443, 52311 (example) depending on the profile.
[03:02] Dr. Maya Rao: 52311? that's oddly specific. can we restrict to 443 only for our hardened environment?
[03:08] Sofia: yes, there is a 443-only profile that routes via a multiplexed websocket. performance is slightly impacted for very high throughput but fine for typical latencies we see — usually sub-200ms token generation for our smaller models. heavy throughput workloads might need dedicated pool.
[03:27] Ethan: this ties into the marketplace listing because if you're listing a 'private packaged' offering we need to validate both the packaging (OVA vs container) and the network profile; marketplace cert teams will ask for both.
[03:38] Jordan: how do we prove performance? the marketplace requires some baseline benchmarks.
[03:43] Noah: we can run a bench on your sandbox. we have a microbenchmark script (often mislabeled 'redwood bench' in some docs) that outputs tokens/sec, p50/p95 latency, and memory footprint. we'll need sample payloads from you to be accurate.
[03:58] Priya: sample payloads will be synthetic. we can't share PHI. we'll provide pseudo-PHI and a redacted prompt set.
[04:07] Sofia: that's fine, the bench only needs representative sequence lengths and concurrency profiles. also, if you're using quantized models on-prem (8-bit, int4), the kernel selection changes and latency numbers shift. we recommend a quantization profile table in the listing docs.
[04:22] Dr. Maya Rao: speaking of models — marketplace listing names the model variants; can we pin to a specific small model for the initial release? we don't want automatic upgrades.
[04:31] Ethan: yes, customers can pin model versions. in Redwood Console you can pin and create rollout policies. for Private, the installed agent respects a pinned model manifest. the one caveat is if you pin to a model that requires a new kernel or a new CUDA lib the installer must bring that dependency or you'll need a minor update.
[04:50] Liam: what's the support SLA for on-prem? we run 24/7 clinical services.
[04:56] Ethan: for enterprise Private we offer an optional premium support add-on with 1-hour on-call and a runbook swap for escalations. that maps to the dedicated capacity and support tiers in the marketplace listing.
[05:08] Jordan: can we discuss the KMS flow? we use an HSM that only does wrapped keys and doesn't allow exporting keys.
[05:15] Sofia: okay, typical flow is: you keep the master key in your HSM/KMS; the Redwood agent uses an ephemeral wrapping key and stores model artifacts encrypted with a data key that is wrapped by your HSM. the control plane only holds wrapped keys and cannot decrypt. we have a reference implementation for AzureKeyVault, AWS KMS, and generic PKCS#11 HSMs. sometimes our docs call PKCS11 without the dash so there are typos in the doc.
[05:39] Priya: PKCS11 is what we need. does Redwood support in-hardware signing for launch attestation? we want to verify runtime integrity.
[05:48] Noah: we have a separate attestation module that can integrate with TPM or HSM attestation for the host. it's not enabled by default but can be included in the on-prem bundle. there's an additional cert step in the installer.
[06:03] Dr. Maya Rao: excellent. any gotchas for air-gapped installs?
[06:09] Sofia: the main items are: initial artifact bootstrap (you must ship model weights via physical media or through an approved s3 import), license checks that normally happen online must be switched to offline licensing, and some of our dependency images will need to be mirrored. we provide an 'airgap-prepare' script. note: in earlier versions it was called 'airgap-prep' so watch for outdated docs.
[06:31] Ethan: i'm going to flag that for marketplace preflight — the listing team will want an air-gap playbook in the listing bundle.
[06:37] Jordan: security review—what evidence do you provide for SOC2?
[06:42] Ethan: we will send SOC2 type II report, encryption at rest/in transit statements, SSO/SAML docs, and our pen test summary. also we'll include the RBAC model for the console.
[06:53] Priya: do you support SCIM for user provisioning?
[06:57] Noah: yes, SCIM and SAML SSO supported; we also support LDAP for on-prem admin sync. sometimes the earlier doc spells it 'SCM' which is wrong—ignore that.
[07:08] Dr. Maya Rao: timeline: we aim to have a pilot running in 6 weeks. is that feasible?
[07:14] Sofia: feasible if we get sandbox access and your infra contacts this week. pilot scope: single on-prem node, model pinned to small LLM, limited concurrency for clinical triage.
[07:25] Liam: can you confirm resource sizing for that node? CPU/GPU needs?
[07:29] Noah: for the small model we recommend a single A40-equivalent or one 80GB GPU if using dense models; if you go quantized CPU-only inference is possible but latency will be higher. we'll provide a sizing matrix.
[07:43] Dr. Maya Rao: cost conversation—do you have an estimate for per-request or per-token when deployed on-prem via marketplace metering?
[07:52] Ethan: with dedicated capacity or marketplace listing there's a couple ways: subscription for the software + per-usage metering optionally. we typically model edge cases in the cost spreadsheet — we'll send an initial estimate for the pilot.
[08:03] Jordan: earlier you mentioned KV cache and batching—if we run multiple concurrent sessions, how do you control memory bloat?
[08:12] Sofia: the runtime supports LRU eviction and configurable KV cache limits. also continuous batching reduces overhead; the tradeoff is slightly higher latency for first token for some requests. if memory is constrained, enable prefix caching and smaller batch windows.
[08:29] Liam: are those knobs exposed in the console or config?
[08:33] Noah: yes both. Console exposes recommended profiles (low-latency, cost-optimized) and the on-prem agent reads a yaml config where you can tune batch window, cache sizes, and quantization policy.
[08:46] Priya: can we get a sample policy for audit and retention to show to our compliance team?
[08:50] Ethan: yes i'll attach a sample retention policy and a sample retention.yaml that maps log types to retention durations.
[08:58] Dr. Maya Rao: anything that blocks marketplace certification?
[09:02] Sofia: the common blockers are: missing air-gap playbook, missing offline license flow, and unclear billing adapter behavior. if we provide all three in the listing bundle we usually clear the preflight.
[09:15] Jordan: do you have a preflight checklist we can review?
[09:18] Noah: yes there's a 'marketplace-preflight-checklist.md' we'll share. it includes test cases and required docs.
[09:24] Ethan: let's talk about pilot instrumentation — we can add tracing hooks to capture model drift indicators and quality regressions during pilot. those are optional but helpful.
[09:36] Dr. Maya Rao: very interested. can that be kept internal? we don't want telemetry leaving the network.
[09:42] Sofia: telemetry for drift is on by default but you can opt out; for pilot we'll set it to local-only and include a periodic export of metrics if you agree.
[09:54] Priya: any PCI/PHI-specific disclaimers we should be aware of?
[09:58] Noah: when processing PHI we recommend disabling cloud caching and ensure logs are routed to your SIEM; also sign a DPA and BA agreement which we can include in the legal packet.
[10:11] Dr. Maya Rao: good. next steps summary?
[10:15] Ethan: recap: 1) we'll share security pack + SOC2 by Tue, 2) Sofia will drop connector compatibility matrix and port matrix, 3) you (Liam) will provision sandbox VPC access and infra contacts, 4) we'll schedule the SE-led pilot kickoff next week.
[10:31] Jordan: sounds good. can you include a sample market listing blurb so our procurement can start the paperwork?
[10:35] Ethan: yes we'll include a draft one-page summary for procurement.
[10:40] Priya: timeline wise, we'll aim to accept documents by 2026-04-07 and try to have sandbox ready by 2026-04-05.
[10:48] Sofia: perfect. one minor note: some of our older docs call the optimize product 'Redwood Optics' which is a typo—ignore references.
[10:58] Ethan: any final questions?
[11:01] Dr. Maya Rao: none from me. thanks.
[11:03] Liam: quick security Q — do you support customer-managed telemetry endpoints over TLS mutual auth?
[11:10] Noah: yes mTLS supported; we can configure client cert rotation to match your PKI cadence.
[11:18] Jordan: ok thanks y'all.
[11:21] Ethan: thanks everyone, we'll follow up with the artifacts. meeting adjourned.

References mentioned: marketplace-preflight-checklist.md, connector-port-matrix, airgap-prepare script, bench script (redwood bench), sample retention.yaml, SOC2 report, DPA/BA.

Action items (restated):
- Ethan: send security pack, SOC2, procurement one-pager (due 2026-04-06)
- Sofia: connector matrix, compatibility notes, sizing matrix (due 2026-04-09)
- Liam (Cobalt): sandbox access + infra contacts (due 2026-04-05)
- Priya (Cobalt): share data residency policy and any special legal constraints (due 2026-04-07)

End of transcript.

title: Opal Foundry — compliance posture readout, Trust Center access and KMS roadmap
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-03-13 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~42 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Priya Shah (Redwood SE), Nina Alvarez (Redwood CSM); Marta Ruiz (Opal Foundry CISO), Daniel Ko (Head Cloud Eng), Aisha Thompson (Compliance)

[00:00] Ethan Park: hey everyone thanks for joining, uh we'll do a quick run through of Opal Foundry's posture questions, uh start with Trust Center access and then go into encryption and KMS flows, is that okay?
[00:08] Marta Ruiz: yes that works — we have a couple of compliance folks on the line so we want to make sure the evidence access story is clear.
[00:15] Daniel Ko: and also, specifically, we need to understand how audit logs flow to our SIEM — retention and export formats, and whether logs are encrypted at rest.
[00:23] Priya Shah: totally, good callout. quick overview: Redwood's Trust Center can be role-scoped read-only for your audit team; we provide per-report download links and an API for automated pulls, plus S3 export for larger bundles. note: the export can be configured to only contain redacted artefacts if needed.
[00:37] Aisha Thompson: when you say role-scoped, do you support SCIM / SAML mapping to our groups so we can limit who can see the SOC artifacts?
[00:45] Nina Alvarez: yes we map SAML attributes to RBAC roles — we have a dedicated "audit_viewer" role that can be assigned via your IdP. SCIM provisioning can be enabled for automated user provisioning.
[00:56] Marta Ruiz: okay are the audit logs in the Trust Center the raw logs or summarized events? we need raw request headers for a couple of audits.
[01:05] Priya Shah: the portal shows summarized events by default, but you can request raw event bundles. there's an API endpoint that returns the raw JSON lines with timestamps, IPs (anonymized on request), and operation codes. small caveat: some fields are redacted by default for PII — we can do a custom pull.
[01:22] Daniel Ko: what about exporter formats — do you support structured JSONLines, Parquet, syslog?
[01:29] Priya Shah: JSONL and Parquet are available. syslog is not first-class, but you can pipe JSONL to your aggregator. we also support streaming via a secure push to a customer-owned S3 bucket in the region you choose.
[01:41] Marta Ruiz: region choice: we need EU-only storage for audit logs for our EU product. does Redwood support per-customer data residency?
[01:49] Ethan Park: yes, we have per-customer region pins. hosted deployments allow you to select region for logs and token-level storage. additionally, for Private deployments we can host the entire control plane in your VPC.
[02:01] Aisha Thompson: about encryption: is data encrypted at rest with customer-managed keys? we need proof that keys are rotated and HSM-backed.
[02:09] Priya Shah: by default we use provider-managed KMS for hosted customers, but we support BYOK: you can supply keys via AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or bring-your-own HSM for Private. rotation policies are configurable and we can provide key rotation logs for audits. note — the HSM path will be part of the KMS integration doc we'll send.
[02:26] Daniel Ko: and for logs forwarded to our SIEM, are they encrypted in transit? TLS mutual auth?
[02:32] Priya Shah: all egress is TLS 1.2+ (we recommend 1.3), and for push to customer S3 we strongly recommend presigned v4 with server-side encryption using your KMS key. mutual TLS is available for dedicated/private networking.
[02:45] Marta Ruiz: can you give specifics on the SOC 2 report — is it Type II, and do you have the period and the scope?
[02:52] Ethan Park: yes we have a SOC 2 Type II covering the period Jul 2025–Dec 2025, scope includes hosting, API, and dedicated runtime features. we will send a redacted copy — security team often redacts customer names and some IP ranges.
[03:05] Aisha Thompson: what's the process for us to get the full report — do we need an NDA?
[03:10] Ethan Park: you can get a redacted copy via Trust Center right away; for the full report we usually require an MSA addendum or an executed NDA, standard process. we'll include the steps in the follow-up email.
[03:20] Daniel Ko: couple of technical details: how does the KV cache look in logs? we want to confirm that no PII is persisted in caching layers.
[03:29] Priya Shah: the KV cache is in-memory and not persisted to disk in hosted mode; we do have optional prefix caching for dedicated deployments which is persisted in encrypted storage — that storage is encrypted and keys can be customer-managed. also our evals team ensures prompt-level PII is stripped unless you opt into caching.
[03:46] Marta Ruiz: okay sounds good. can Redwood create an evidence snapshot for a point-in-time for auditors? that's a big ask for us.
[03:54] Nina Alvarez: yes — we can generate time-bound evidence bundles (we call them snapshots) that include config, logs for the interval, RBAC snapshots, and policy artefacts. on request, snapshots can be delivered to a customer-owned S3 with MFA-delete enabled.
[04:11] Daniel Ko: about RBAC — do roles map to API keys or SSO identities? what's the best practice for service accounts?
[04:18] Priya Shah: roles map to IdP assertions for human users; for machine/service accounts we recommend ephemeral API tokens issued by Redwood with short TTL and scoped permissions. we also support long-lived service credentials if you require them but we discourage that for security.
[04:31] Marta Ruiz: can we get log-level access for a specific token — like token-level audit to see which model version served a request?
[04:38] Priya Shah: yes token-level telemetry exists — you can see model, sequence length, latency, region, and request attributes. we retain token-level metrics for the retention window you choose; raw request bodies are retained by default for 7 days unless you set a different retention policy.
[04:55] Aisha Thompson: we will need 90-day retention for raw request audit and 7-year for summarized compliance metrics, is that workable?
[05:03] Ethan Park: summarized metrics we can retain for multi-year; raw request retention is configurable but larger windows have cost and privacy implications. we'll propose a retention plan in the follow-up that maps to your SLA.
[05:15] Daniel Ko: on the private deployment route: if we go on-prem, is the control plane still Redwood-managed or do we get a full handoff?
[05:23] Nina Alvarez: for Private options, there's a spectrum — VPC-hosted control plane with customer network boundary or fully air-gapped install. fully air-gapped is possible but requires a deeper professional services engagement.
[05:35] Marta Ruiz: for our auditors, we need evidence that key rotation happened and no key material left Redwood. can you provide key rotation logs and attestations?
[05:44] Priya Shah: yes, we export KMS audit logs — rotation events, who initiated, key version IDs. for HSM deployments we can provide attestation documents and the HSM vendor's certificate of compliance.
[05:56] Daniel Ko: any limitations on field-level redaction? e.g., can we ask to redact name/email, but keep transaction id?
[06:04] Priya Shah: we support custom redaction at ingest time — we configure filters that keep operational fields like txn_id and strip PII. we'll need to align on a few sample payloads so the filters behave correctly.
[06:16] Marta Ruiz: what about pen tests and bug bounty reports — do you share summaries in Trust Center?
[06:22] Ethan Park: high-level pen test summaries and remediation timelines are in Trust Center. vulnerability details are redacted; severity, CVE if public, and remediation status are visible. for customers under an NDA we can provide more detail.
[06:34] Aisha Thompson: quick question on SSO — do you support multiple IdPs or SAML + OIDC mixed environments? we have legacy apps on SAML and new ones on OIDC.
[06:42] Nina Alvarez: yes we support multiple IdPs; you can attach SAML & OIDC providers and map claims to roles. we also support step-up authentication policies.
[06:51] Daniel Ko: we have a final question on latency vs. encryption overhead — does enabling customer-managed KMS add latency to inference?
[06:58] Priya Shah: encryption at rest and key operations happen at the control plane and during storage operations, not on the inference hot path. fetching ephemeral per-request secrets can add minimal overhead if you do per-request decrypt; we support cached data keys (envelope encryption) to avoid per-request KMS latency.
[07:14] Marta Ruiz: great — let's recap next steps.
[07:18] Ethan Park: recap: Redwood sends redacted SOC 2 Type II via Trust Center by Monday; SE (Priya) will deliver KMS/HSM integration diagram and network flow by 2026-03-20; customer confirms residency and retention by 2026-03-18; schedule follow-up private deploy demo week of 2026-03-23.
[07:34] Marta Ruiz: that works, we'll also prepare sample payloads for redaction testing.
[07:39] Ethan Park: perfect — we'll circle back once the docs are shared. thanks everyone.

Summary/Notes:
- Trust Center supports role-scoped read-only access, SAML/SCIM provisioning, API exports (JSONL/Parquet), and S3 push.
- KMS options: provider-managed by default, BYOK with AWS/GCP KMS, HSM-backed for Private deployments; rotation logs and attestations available.
- Logs: raw and summarized exports available; retention configurable; region pinning supported for EU-only storage.
- RBAC: IdP claim mapping + ephemeral tokens recommended for service accounts.
- Evidence snapshots available and can be delivered to customer-owned S3 with MFA-delete.

Action items (from transcript):
- Priya Shah: KMS/HSM integration diagram and HSM attestation checklist by 2026-03-20.
- Ethan Park: share redacted SOC 2 Type II and ISO readout via Trust Center link by 2026-03-15.
- Marta Ruiz: confirm EU residency constraints and retention windows by 2026-03-18.

End of transcript.

title: Acme  Private upgrade escalation (rollback keeps re-triggering)
preview:
Meeting header
- Date: 2026-02-14
- Time: 1:00 PM PT
- Duration: 41 minutes
- Attendees: Ava Chen, Ethan Park, Nina Shah, Omar Raza

Summary
Customer reports that the Private upgrade is stuck and rollback keeps re-triggering. Redwood shares a workaround involving deleting a rollback lock ConfigMap and restarting the installer controller. Discussed guardrails and confirmation steps.

Transcript
[00:00] Ava Chen: Thanks for jumping on. We saw your email thread. Want to make sure we get you unblocked today.
[00:18] Nina Shah: Yeah, its been uh rolling back since last night. The UI just says rollback in progress, and it never ends.
[00:35] Ethan Park: Got it. Weve seen a similar failure mode. In logs do you see it repeating like scheduling rollback retry?
[00:52] Omar Raza: Exactly. Its like it fails, waits thirty seconds, and then does it again.
[01:10] Ava Chen: Okay. There is a workaround but it involves a couple kubectl commands. Are you comfortable running those?
[01:22] Nina Shah: Yep, thats fine, just need to be sure its safe.
[01:35] Ethan Park: The key is: only delete the specific configmap called `installer-rollback-lock` in the `redwood-private` namespace. Dont touch anything else.
[02:01] Omar Raza: And then restart the controller deployment?
[02:05] Ethan Park: Yeah, `rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`. That should allow it to complete and stop the loop.
[02:24] Nina Shah: Is this a known bug?
[02:30] Ethan Park: Yes, tracked internally. Sometimes youll hear us call it a rollback loop or stuck rollback. Were working on a patch so it cant happen again.
[03:10] Ava Chen: After you run it, can you paste back the log lines around the time it transitions?
[03:22] Nina Shah: Sure.
[05:10] Omar Raza: Just to confirm, were currently on 2.8.0 trying 2.9.0.
[05:18] Ethan Park: Thats consistent with what weve seen.
[38:40] Ava Chen: Recap: run the two commands, send us the logs, and well confirm patch timing for 2.9.1. Thanks everyone.

title: Vertex LegalTech - audit log latency escalation (delivery lag + interim mitigation)
preview: Vertex escalated that audit logs in their Redwood Private/VPC deployment are arriving 30–45 minutes late, causing gaps in their compliance monitoring and alerting. Redwood confirmed the audit-log pipeline can backlog under specific load patterns and that there is currently no customer-visible lag metric/alert. The team aligned on expectations (targeting single-digit minutes, with defined thresholds by tier), immediate mitigations (widen export window, implement retries/backoff, avoid treating the feed as real-time until fixed), and an engineering plan tied to SUP-1846 / ENG-4824 to add a first-class lag metric + alerting + runbook updates. Rachel owned customer comms cadence and a written mitigation note; SRE owned confirming current backlog status and a timeline for the alert rollout.

text: Internal walkthrough of the end-to-end marketplace onboarding flow to validate the UX and docs before submission. Team identified confusion around org linking for existing Redwood orgs, unclear messaging during entitlement propagation delays (AWS especially), and missing docs for the "already subscribed" and "multiple subscriptions" edge cases. Engineering confirmed expected entitlement API error codes and noted where console should map them to actionable retry guidance. Docs team flagged needed screenshots and a clearer separation of AWS vs GCP steps, plus a single canonical "How marketplace billing maps to usage" section to reduce support tickets.
